**The Kerala State Government plans to introduce an *AI-powered, real-time crowd-density monitoring and alert system* for the Sabarimala pilgrimage season. As a design consultant, identify and analyze the drivers that will govern the design of this system.**

#### **(a) Identify at least six drivers across different categories and justify their relevance to the project. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Driver | Category | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crowd density at critical bottleneck points** (Sannidhanam, Pampa, Appam-Medam) | User / Safety | Real-time density > 4 persons/m² triggers stampede risk; must be detected |
| 2 | **Integration with existing Kerala Police command center** | Technology | Driver for API compatibility and real-time data streaming |
| 3 | **Compliance with Kerala Police Cyberdome data security norms** | Regulatory | Pilgrim data (CCTV, Aadhaar-linked passes) must comply with IT Act 2000 and DPDP Act 2023 |
| 4 | **Power reliability in remote forest terrain** | Environmental | Forest location has unreliable grid; system must run on solar + battery |
| 5 | **Affordability of deployment across 200+ checkpoints** | Economic | Per-unit cost must be below ₹50,000 for scale; driver for low-cost edge AI |
| 6 | **Multilingual alert dissemination (Malayalam, Tamil, Hindi, English, Telugu)** | Cultural | Pilgrims are multi-lingual; alerts must be comprehensible to all |
| 7 | **Seasonal scalability (only used 2 months/year)** | Business | Driver for modular, redeployable hardware to optimize CapEx |

**Synthesis:** These drivers span **safety, technology, regulation, environment, economy, culture, and business** — a balanced PESTLE + user-driven analysis.

---

#### **(b) Construct a Stakeholder Power–Interest Grid for this project. Explain how it informs driver prioritization. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stakeholder Identification & Grid Placement:**

| Stakeholder | Power | Interest | Quadrant | Strategic Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kerala State Police & SDMA | High | High | **Q1: Manage Closely** | Their drivers (crowd safety, command-control integration) are *primary* |
| Travancore Devaswom Board | High | High | **Q1: Manage Closely** | Their drivers (pilgrim experience, traditional sanctity) are *primary* |
| Ministry of Home Affairs (GoI) | High | Low | **Q2: Keep Satisfied** | Their drivers (national security, anti-terror surveillance) are *secondary* |
| Kerala State Electricity Board | High | Low | **Q2: Keep Satisfied** | Their drivers (grid load management) are *secondary* |
| Pilgrims & Devotee Associations | Low | High | **Q3: Keep Informed** | Their drivers (ease of darshan, safety reassurance) inform *user experience* drivers |
| Local Panchayats & Vendors | Low | High | **Q3: Keep Informed** | Their drivers (livelihood, traffic) inform *deployment* drivers |
| General Media | Low | Low | **Q4: Monitor** | Their drivers (transparency) are *passive monitoring* concerns |

**How the Grid Informs Driver Prioritization:**

1. **Q1 stakeholders** generate the **top-priority drivers**: real-time safety alerts, command-center integration, pilgrim-flow optimization.
2. **Q2 stakeholders** generate **constraint drivers**: power stability, regulatory compliance.
3. **Q3 stakeholders** generate **experience drivers**: multilingual alerts, accessibility.
4. **Q4 stakeholders** generate **reputation drivers**: transparency, public communication.

**Conclusion:** The design team must first resolve Q1 drivers (safety + administration), then layer Q2 constraints (power + regulation), and finally polish with Q3 user experience and Q4 reputation drivers. This sequence ensures **a deployable, compliant, and accepted system**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Identifying Drivers**

- ☐ **Definition:** Drivers are the *forces that shape* a design project — they answer *why, for whom, against what constraints*.
- ☐ **Seven Categories:** User, Business, Technology, Sustainability, Regulatory, Cultural, Economic — memorize at least **5 by name and example**.
- ☐ **PESTLE** is the *primary macro-driver framework* — Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental.
- ☐ **Stakeholder Mapping** must precede driver identification — no stakeholders = no drivers.
- ☐ **Primary Research** (interviews, observation) and **Secondary Research** (literature, reports) are *both* essential — never use only one.
- ☐ **5-Whys Analysis** distinguishes *root drivers* from *symptom drivers* — always go deeper.
- ☐ **Driver Trees** decompose root → branch → leaf; great for descriptive marks in exams.
- ☐ **Priority Matrix** uses the formula $P_i = I_i \times L_i$ — practice scoring at least one example.
- ☐ **Symptom vs Driver:** *"Customers are unhappy"* (symptom) vs *"Product fails to address ergonomic need"* (driver).
- ☐ **Iterate:** Drivers are *not static* — revisit after prototyping in Module 3+.
- ☐ **Cite Examples:** Kerala-based examples (Kerala floods, Sabarimala, smallholder farming) score higher than generic global examples.
- ☐ **Draw at least one diagram** (PESTLE, Driver Tree, or Stakeholder Grid) — it earns **automatic descriptive marks**.
- ☐ **Avoid:** Listing drivers without categorization, missing prioritization, no real-world example, no diagram.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Identifying Drivers | PECNT637**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Gathering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT637 - DESIGN PROCESS AND PERSPECTIVE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Information Gathering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Gathering** is the second phase of the Design Process wherein designers systematically collect, organize, and synthesize raw data about users, their behaviors, needs, pain points, the operational context, competitive landscape, and technological constraints to build an evidence-based foundation for ideation and concept development.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Outcome-Based Education under NEP 2020), Information Gathering is positioned as a *pre-ideation* activity that bridges the *Empathize* stage of Design Thinking with the *Define* stage. It is the disciplined acquisition of **qualitative** and **quantitative** data that reduces designer assumptions, mitigates cognitive bias, and grounds the eventual solution in real-world user realities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 of PECNT637 (Design Process and Perspective) treats Information Gathering as the *evidence-generation* engine of the design lifecycle. It directly supports **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Conduct user and market research to frame design problems with empathy and evidence."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Detective at a Crime Scene Analogy**
> Imagine a detective arriving at a crime scene. Before interrogating suspects, the detective *gathers evidence* — photographs the scene, collects fingerprints, interviews bystanders, reviews CCTV footage, and studies past case files. The detective does NOT jump to conclusions about "whodunit" without first gathering facts. **Information Gathering in design is exactly this**: the designer is a detective, the user is the crime scene, and the eventual product is the verdict. Skipping evidence collection leads to a flawed conviction (failed product).

**Another simpler analogy:** Think of Information Gathering as the *foundation digging* before constructing a building. The deeper, wider, and truer the foundation (information), the stronger and longer-lasting the structure (product). A skyscraper built on a shallow assumption-based foundation will eventually collapse.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

The following terms are **standardized across KTU design syllabi** and must be mastered for the ESE:

- **Primary Research** — Data collected *firsthand* by the designer from end users, stakeholders, or environments.
- **Secondary Research** — Data reused from *existing sources* (journals, reports, patents, databases).
- **Qualitative Data** — Non-numerical insights describing *why* and *how* (interviews, observations).
- **Quantitative Data** — Numerical insights describing *how many* and *how much* (surveys, analytics).
- **Ethnography** — Deep, immersive, long-duration field study of users in their natural environment.
- **Sample Size (n)** — The **number of participants** in a research study. A robust qualitative study typically uses **n = 5 to 30**; a robust quantitative study typically uses **n ≥ 100** for statistical confidence.
- **Saturation Point** — The moment in qualitative research when *no new themes* emerge from additional interviews (typically reached at **n ≈ 12 to 15** for a homogeneous user group).
- **Empathy Map** — A 2x2 visual tool capturing what the user *Says, Thinks, Does, and Feels*.
- **Persona** — A fictional, research-grounded archetype representing a user segment.
- **Triangulation** — Cross-verification of findings using **at least three** independent data sources or methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reminder:** The KTU 2024 examiner frequently tests the *distinction between primary and secondary research* and the *selection of appropriate method for a given design problem*. Memorize the difference, not just the names.

---

## 1.4 Position of Information Gathering in the Design Lifecycle

$$\text{Empathize} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Information Gathering}} \rightarrow \text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Ideate} \rightarrow \text{Prototype} \rightarrow \text{Test}$$

The phase is **iterative**, not linear — findings often force a return to the Empathize stage for deeper inquiry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Three Pillars of Information

Information in design is classified into **three orthogonal pillars**. Every KTU design problem requires data from all three:

### Pillar A — User Data
- **Who** is the user? (demographics, psychographics)
- **What** do they do? (behavior)
- **Why** do they do it? (motivation)
- **What** do they struggle with? (pain points)
- **What** do they desire? (gains, jobs-to-be-done)

### Pillar B — Context Data
- **Where** is the product used? (physical, digital, social environment)
- **When** is it used? (time, frequency, duration)
- **How** is the surrounding ecosystem structured? (regulations, culture, infrastructure)

### Pillar C — Domain Data
- **What** competing or analogous solutions exist? (benchmarking)
- **What** technological, material, or manufacturing constraints apply? (feasibility)
- **What** are the historical, cultural, or regulatory precedents? (standards)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all three?** A designer who knows the user but ignores technical feasibility will propose a product that cannot be built. A designer who knows the technology but ignores the user will build a product no one wants. **Triangulation across the three pillars** is the hallmark of a mature KTU answer.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Information Gathering Methods

### 2.2.1 Primary Research Methods (Firsthand)

| Method | Type | Sample Size | When to Use | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **One-on-One Interview** | Qualitative | 5–15 | Deep individual insights | User narratives, pain quotes |
| **Focus Group** | Qualitative | 6–10 per group | Group dynamics, social norms | Themes, controversial views |
| **Direct Observation** | Qualitative | 3–10 contexts | Actual behavior vs stated behavior | Behavioral patterns, workarounds |
| **Ethnographic Study** | Qualitative | 1–5 users, days/weeks | Deep cultural/contextual insights | Rich contextual narratives |
| **Survey / Questionnaire** | Quantitative | $\geq$ 100 | Statistical validation, breadth | Frequency distributions, trends |
| **Diary Study** | Qualitative | 5–20 users, 1–4 weeks | Longitudinal behavior tracking | Time-stamped user logs |
| **Contextual Inquiry** | Qualitative | 5–10 | Combining interview + observation in real setting | Task models, artifact maps |
| **Usability Test (Gen 0)** | Mixed | 5–8 | Early concept validation | Task success rates, error counts |

### 2.2.2 Secondary Research Methods (Existing Data)

| Method | Type | Source Examples | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Literature Review** | Qualitative | Academic journals, conference papers | Theoretical grounding |
| **Market Reports** | Quantitative | Statista, McKinsey, IBISWorld | Market sizing, trends |
| **Patent Search** | Mixed | USPTO, WIPO, Google Patents | Technology landscape, IP freedom |
| **Benchmarking** | Mixed | Competitor products, tear-downs | Performance baselines |
| **Web/Social Analytics** | Quantitative | Google Analytics, social listening tools | Digital behavior patterns |
| **Regulatory Review** | Qualitative | BIS, ISO, ASTM, IEC standards | Compliance constraints |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations govern quantitative information gathering. They are examinable.**

### Equation 1: Sample Size for Finite Population (Cochran's Formula)

$$n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)}{e^2}$$

Where:
- $n_0$ = required sample size
- $Z$ = Z-score for confidence level (**1.96** for 95% confidence, **2.576** for 99%)
- $p$ = estimated population proportion (**0.5** used when unknown — most conservative)
- $e$ = margin of error (typically **0.05**)

**Correction for finite population** (when $N$ is known and $n_0 \vert N > 5\%$):

$$n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$$

### Equation 2: Survey Response Rate

$$R = \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{N_{\text{distributed}}} \times 100\%$$

A **healthy** response rate is $\geq 60\%$; below **30%** indicates sampling bias.

### Equation 3: Confidence Interval for a Mean

$$CI = \bar{x} \pm Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Where $\bar{x}$ = sample mean, $\sigma$ = population standard deviation, $n$ = sample size.

### Equation 4: Task Completion Rate (Usability)

$$TCR = \frac{N_{\text{completed tasks}}}{N_{\text{attempted tasks}}} \times 100\%$$

KTU benchmark: $TCR \geq 78\%$ indicates a usable concept.

### Equation 5: Nielsen's Qualitative Sample Saturation Heuristic

$$N_{\text{saturation}} \approx 5 \times U_{\text{segments}}$$

Where $U_{\text{segments}}$ is the number of distinct user segments. Beyond this, new interviews yield $< 1\%$ new insights.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Information Gathering |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Design** | Contextual inquiry of daily commute to design ergonomic interiors |
| **Medical Devices** | Ethnographic studies in ICUs to minimize clinician cognitive load |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Diary studies tracking smartphone usage to inform battery UX |
| **Civil Engineering** | Stakeholder interviews with residents for public infrastructure design |
| **Software / UX** | A/B testing analytics for iterative UI refinement |
| **Furniture Design** | Home visits to study anthropometrics and living space constraints |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connect:** For KTU multidisciplinary design projects, Information Gathering often involves **technical benchmarking** (e.g., load testing, material analysis reports) blended with **user research**. Distinguish these in your answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cochran's Sample Size Formula

The derivation begins with the assumption that the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is approximately normally distributed by the **Central Limit Theorem** for sufficiently large $n$.

### Step 1 — Confidence Interval Setup

For a population mean with known variance $\sigma^2$, the standardized statistic is:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

This $Z$ follows the standard normal distribution $N(0, 1)$.

### Step 2 — Imposing the Confidence Requirement

For a $(1 - \alpha)$ confidence interval, we require:

$$P\!\left(\, \left\vert \bar{X} - \mu \right\vert \leq e \,\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Substituting the Z-statistic:

$$P\!\left(\, \left\vert Z \right\vert \leq \frac{e \sqrt{n}}{\sigma} \,\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

### Step 3 — Solving for n

For a proportion (binary outcome), the variance is $p(1 - p)$, and solving for $n$ yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
e^2 &= \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1 - p)}{n} \\[4pt]
n_0 &= \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1 - p)}{e^2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Finite Population Correction

If the sample exceeds 5% of the population, the variance of the sample mean decreases. The corrected formula becomes:

$$n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$$

**Derivation note:** The denominator arises because sampling without replacement reduces the variance by the factor $\frac{N - n}{N - 1}$ (hypergeometric correction).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Sample Size Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A design team is conducting a survey to validate a new ergonomic chair concept. They want **95% confidence**, a margin of error of **0.05**, and have no prior estimate of the proportion (use $p = 0.5$). The total user population of interest is **$N = 800$** office workers.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= 1.96 \quad (\text{95\% confidence}) \\
p &= 0.5 \quad (\text{no prior estimate}) \\
e &= 0.05 \\
N &= 800
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial sample size $n_0$.**

$$n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(0.05)^2}$$

$$n_0 = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.0025} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025} = 384.16$$

**Step 3 — Apply finite population correction.**

$$n = \frac{384.16}{1 + \dfrac{384.16 - 1}{800}} = \frac{384.16}{1 + 0.479} = \frac{384.16}{1.479} \approx 259.74$$

**Step 4 — Round up to integer.**

$$\boxed{n = 260 \text{ respondents}}$$

**Answer:** The team must survey at least **260 office workers** to achieve the desired statistical confidence.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Task Completion Rate Analysis

**Problem:** A design team tested a new ATM interface prototype with **8 users**. Each user attempted **5 cash withdrawal tasks**. The team recorded the following completed-task data: 4, 5, 3, 5, 4, 5, 4, 5. Determine the average TCR and compare against the KTU benchmark.

**Step 1 — Compute individual TCRs.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TCR_1 &= \tfrac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80\% \\
TCR_2 &= \tfrac{5}{5} \times 100 = 100\% \\
TCR_3 &= \tfrac{3}{5} \times 100 = 60\% \\
TCR_4 &= \tfrac{5}{5} \times 100 = 100\% \\
TCR_5 &= \tfrac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80\% \\
TCR_6 &= \tfrac{5}{5} \times 100 = 100\% \\
TCR_7 &= \tfrac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80\% \\
TCR_8 &= \tfrac{5}{5} \times 100 = 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean TCR.**

$$\overline{TCR} = \frac{80 + 100 + 60 + 100 + 80 + 100 + 80 + 100}{8} = \frac{700}{8} = 87.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare against KTU benchmark of 78%.**

Since $87.5\% > 78\%$, the prototype passes the KTU usability threshold.

**Step 4 — Identify outlier.**

User 3 (60%) is an outlier. The team should conduct a **retrospective interview** with User 3 to understand failure points.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method Selection Decision Tree (Textual)

When a KTU problem asks *"Which information gathering method is most appropriate?"* use the following logical sequence:

1. **Is the question about WHY or HOW-MANY?**
   - WHY → **Qualitative** methods (interview, observation, ethnography)
   - HOW-MANY → **Quantitative** methods (survey, analytics)

2. **Is the user behavior conscious and articulable, or unconscious?**
   - Conscious/Articulable → **Interview, Survey**
   - Unconscious/Habitual → **Observation, Ethnography**

3. **Is the context controllable (lab) or natural (field)?**
   - Controllable → **Usability test, Focus group**
   - Natural → **Contextual inquiry, Diary study, Ethnography**

4. **Is the timeline short (< 1 week) or long (weeks–months)?**
   - Short → **Interviews, Surveys**
   - Long → **Diary studies, Ethnographic immersion**

5. **Is the goal to generate ideas or to validate them?**
   - Generate → **Empathy interviews, Observation** (divergent)
   - Validate → **Surveys, Usability tests** (convergent)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most students lose marks by *defaulting to "conduct a survey"* without justifying method selection. Always articulate the **decision logic** from the problem constraints to the chosen method. This is worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Synthesis: From Raw Data to Design Insight

Information Gathering is not complete when data is collected. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires **synthesis**. Use the **Affinity Diagram** process:

$$\text{Raw Data (Quotes, Observations, Metrics)} \rightarrow \text{Notes} \rightarrow \text{Clusters (Themes)} \rightarrow \text{Insights} \rightarrow \text{Design Opportunities}$$

Each transition requires **designer interpretation**, not mechanical sorting. An *insight* is a non-obvious, actionable truth about the user (e.g., *"Users do not avoid smartwatches because of cost; they avoid them because charging them disrupts their sleep ritual"*).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: The Information Gathering Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Brief / Problem Statement] --> B[Define Research Objectives]
    B --> C{Data Type Needed?}
    C -->|Qualitative| D[Plan Qualitative Methods]
    C -->|Quantitative| E[Plan Quantitative Methods]
    C -->|Both| F[Plan Mixed Methods]
    D --> G[Select Sample and Recruit]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Execute Primary Research]
    H --> I[Conduct Secondary Research]
    I --> J[Triangulate Cross-Verify Data]
    J --> K[Synthesize via Affinity Mapping]
    K --> L[Generate Insights and Personas]
    L --> M[Document Research Findings]
    M --> N[Hand-off to Define Stage]
    N --> O{Sufficient Evidence?}
    O -->|No| B
    O -->|Yes| P[Proceed to Problem Definition]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Method Classification Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PRIMARY
        P1[Interviews]
        P2[Focus Groups]
        P3[Observation]
        P4[Ethnography]
        P5[Surveys]
        P6[Diary Studies]
        P7[Usability Tests]
    end
    subgraph SECONDARY
        S1[Literature Review]
        S2[Market Reports]
        S3[Patent Search]
        S4[Benchmarking]
        S5[Web Analytics]
        S6[Regulatory Standards]
    end
    subgraph QUALITATIVE
        P1
        P2
        P3
        P4
        P6
        S1
        S3
        S6
    end
    subgraph QUANTITATIVE
        P5
        P7
        S2
        S5
    end
    P1 -.-> T((Triangulation))
    P5 -.-> T
    S2 -.-> T
    T --> OUT[Validated Design Brief]
```

## 4.3 Empathy Map — Visual Synthesis Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EMPATHY_MAP[Empathy Map for a Single User Segment]
        direction TB
        Q1[SAYS - Direct Quotes] --- Q2[THINKS - Beliefs, Concerns]
        Q2 --- Q3[DOES - Observable Actions]
        Q3 --- Q4[FEELS - Emotions, Fears]
    end
    EM[Raw Research Data] --> EMPATHY_MAP
    EMPATHY_MAP --> PM[Persona Generation]
    PM --> JTBD[Jobs To Be Done Framework]
    JTBD --> HMW[How Might We Questions]
    HMW --> IDEATE[Ideation Stage]
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram: Stakeholder Interaction in Research

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DS as Designer
    participant US as User
    participant ST as Stakeholder Client
    participant DB as Secondary Database

    DS->>US: 1. Recruit and consent
    US-->>DS: 2. Interview responses
    DS->>US: 3. Observe in context
    US-->>DS: 4. Behavioral data
    DS->>ST: 5. Validate requirements
    ST-->>DS: 6. Business constraints
    DS->>DB: 7. Query secondary data
    DB-->>DS: 8. Market and patent data
    DS->>DS: 9. Triangulate and synthesize
    DS-->>ST: 10. Research report with insights
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Primary Research and Secondary Research. Give two examples of each in the context of product design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Research | Secondary Research |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Data collected firsthand by the designer for the current problem | Data reused from existing sources compiled by others |
| **Cost & Time** | High cost, high time | Low cost, low time |
| **Specificity** | Highly specific to current problem | Generic, may need reinterpretation |
| **Examples** | (1) User interviews, (2) Field observation | (1) Market reports, (2) Patent databases |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tabular contrast, 1 mark for contextual examples.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is an Empathy Map? List its four quadrants and explain why it is used in information gathering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Empathy Map** is a 2x2 visual synthesis tool that captures a user segment's perspective across four quadrants:

1. **SAYS** — Direct quotes the user utters during research
2. **THINKS** — Internal beliefs, worries, and aspirations
3. **DOES** — Observable actions and behaviors
4. **FEELS** — Emotional states (frustrations, joys, fears)

It is used in information gathering to **consolidate disparate qualitative data into a single visual artifact**, ensuring the design team builds a *shared, holistic understanding* of the user before ideating. It prevents selective interpretation and makes hidden user tensions visible. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for four quadrants, 1 for rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A design team is developing a smart water bottle for elderly users (aged 65+) living independently. The product must remind users to hydrate and alert caregivers in case of emergencies.**
**(a)** Propose a comprehensive **Information Gathering plan** for this project. Justify your method selection using at least **three primary** and **two secondary** research methods. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the **minimum sample size** for the user survey assuming 95% confidence, 5% margin of error, $p = 0.5$, and a total elderly population of $N = 2000$ in the target locality. Apply finite population correction and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Research Objectives (1 mark)**

The information gathering must answer:
- What are the daily hydration habits of elderly users?
- What physical, cognitive, and technological barriers exist?
- What is the caregiver's role and information need?
- What competing products exist in the market?

**Step 2 — Select Primary Methods (4 marks)**

| Method | Justification |
|---|---|
| **Contextual Inquiry in Homes** | Elderly users may not accurately self-report; observing them in their kitchen and living room reveals actual habits, cup sizes used, and forgetfulness patterns. Combines interview + observation for high validity. |
| **Semi-Structured Interviews with Caregivers** | Caregivers are secondary stakeholders; their needs (alert fatigue, response protocols) are critical and cannot be inferred from user interviews alone. |
| **Diary Study (2 weeks)** | Hydration is a longitudinal behavior; a diary captures daily variability, time-of-day patterns, and contextual triggers (meals, medication) that one-shot interviews miss. |

**Step 3 — Select Secondary Methods (2 marks)**

| Method | Justification |
|---|---|
| **Gerontology Literature Review** | Provides established data on age-related decline in thirst perception, cognitive load, and medication interactions. |
| **Patent and Product Benchmarking** | Identifies prior art (e.g., HidrateSpark, Thermos Smart Lid) to avoid IP infringement and learn from existing feature sets. |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$Z = 1.96, \quad p = 0.5, \quad e = 0.05, \quad N = 2000$$

**Step 2 — Compute $n_0$.**

$$n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025} = 384.16$$

**Step 3 — Apply finite population correction.**

$$n = \frac{384.16}{1 + \dfrac{384.16 - 1}{2000}} = \frac{384.16}{1 + 0.1916} = \frac{384.16}{1.1916} \approx 322.4$$

**Step 4 — Round up.**

$$\boxed{n = 323 \text{ elderly users}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 marks).**

The required sample is 323. Since this is **16.15%** of the population (323/2000), the finite correction is justified. The team should account for **attrition** in elderly studies (recommended +20% buffer) and plan for approximately **388 outreach contacts** to achieve 323 valid responses. The design team should also conduct **3–5 contextual inquiries** and **8–10 caregiver interviews** as the qualitative complement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A startup is designing a low-cost neonatal incubator for rural Indian hospitals.**
**(a)** Discuss the **role of ethnography and contextual inquiry** in gathering information for this project. What specific data would these methods reveal that surveys cannot? **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Construct a **research synthesis framework** showing how raw field data can be converted into actionable design insights, citing at least two synthesis tools. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Ethnography and Contextual Inquiry — Why They Are Indispensable (5 marks)**

Rural neonatal care is governed by **hidden system dynamics** that surveys cannot penetrate:

1. **Infrastructure Realities** — Ethnographic immersion reveals actual power availability (often 2–4 hours/day), voltage fluctuations, dust levels, ambient temperature ranges, and the presence/absence of trained staff during night shifts. A survey asking "Do you have 24x7 electricity?" yields a *yes/no*, missing the *quality* of power.

2. **Workflow Disruptions** — Contextual inquiry shows how nurses *actually* move between multiple infants, the cognitive load of manual temperature monitoring, and the unspoken **workarounds** (e.g., placing water bottles near the heater for humidity).

3. **Cultural and Family Dynamics** — Ethnography surfaces family rituals around newborn care (e.g., wrapping, oil massage, naming ceremonies) that influence when the incubator can be opened for parental access.

4. **Supply Chain Constraints** — Direct observation reveals that consumables (sterile tubing, distilled water) are frequently out of stock, dictating the design of refillable or disposable alternatives.

5. **Stakeholder Map** — Ethnography identifies overlooked stakeholders: the *ayah* (helper), the sweeper, the local electrician — each interacting with the incubator in ways that affect safety and maintenance.

**What Surveys Cannot Reveal (2 marks)**

Surveys capture *stated* preferences and self-reported frequencies. They fail to capture **latent needs**, **tacit knowledge**, **behavioral inconsistencies**, and **contextual constraints**. For life-critical medical devices, the gap between *what people say* and *what they do* is a patient-safety risk.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Research Synthesis Framework (5 marks)**

The framework follows a four-stage pipeline:

$$\text{Raw Data} \rightarrow \text{Coded Notes} \rightarrow \text{Themed Clusters} \rightarrow \text{Design Insights} \rightarrow \text{Design Opportunities}$$

| Stage | Activity | Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Capture | Audio/video, field notes, photographs | Field journal, camera |
| 2. Code | Tag each datum with descriptive labels | Color-coded sticky notes |
| 3. Cluster | Group codes into thematic affinity clusters | **Affinity Diagram** |
| 4. Synthesize | Translate clusters into user-need statements | **Personas, Empathy Maps** |

**Two Synthesis Tools in Detail (2 marks)**

1. **Affinity Diagram** — Physical or digital clustering of post-it notes into related groups, revealing latent patterns. For the incubator project, clusters might emerge around *"Power instability," "Staff training gaps," "Parental anxiety,"* and *"Hygiene protocol deviations."*

2. **Jobs-to-Be-Done (JTBD) Framework** — Reframes findings as *"When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]."* Example: *"When a nurse is alone on night shift with 6 critical infants, I want the incubator to auto-alert me on temperature drift, so I can prioritize the most at-risk baby."*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** list information gathering methods without **justification** for the specific problem. Generic lists score < 50% of available marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the *Empathize* stage (purely human-centered observation) with the *Information Gathering* stage (broader, including technical, market, and regulatory data). KTU specifically tests this distinction.
> - **Do NOT** use qualitative methods (e.g., "we will interview 500 users"). Mismatched scale is a frequent error.
> - **Always** state **assumptions** (population size, confidence level, margin of error) explicitly when solving sample size problems. Missing assumptions = lost marks.
> - **Always** show the **finite population correction step** even if $n_0 \vert N < 5\%$. Examiners award marks for the *process*, not just the final number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Information Gathering (Module 2)**

- **Definition** — Systematic, evidence-based collection of user, context, and domain data to ground design decisions.
- **Three Pillars** — User Data + Context Data + Domain Data = complete information coverage.
- **Two Families of Research** — Primary (firsthand) vs Secondary (existing). Choose based on time, budget, and specificity needs.
- **Two Data Types** — Qualitative (*why/how*) vs Quantitative (*how many*). Mature projects use **mixed methods**.
- **Sample Size** — Use **Cochran's formula** for proportions, apply **finite population correction** when $n_0 \vert N > 5\%$.
- **Confidence Levels** — 95% → $Z = 1.96$; 99% → $Z = 2.576$.
- **Saturation** — Qualitative studies reach saturation at $\approx 5 \times$ user segments.
- **Key Synthesis Tools** — Affinity Diagram, Empathy Map, Persona, Journey Map, Jobs-to-Be-Done.
- **Triangulation** — Always cross-validate using **at least three independent sources** for robust findings.
- **Position in Design Process** — Sits between Empathize and Define; **iterative**, not one-shot.
- **Common Student Error** — Defaulting to surveys. **Surveys are blunt instruments** for early-stage design; use them only after qualitative themes are established.
- **Documentation** — Every research activity must produce an artifact (transcript, photo, dataset, insight card) for KTU project evaluation.
- **Ethics** — Informed consent, anonymization, and right-to-withdraw are **mandatory** for primary research involving human participants.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Groups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT637 - DESIGN PROCESS AND PERSPECTIVE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Target Groups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design Process and Perspective**, a **Target Group** (also referred to as a *target audience*, *user segment*, or *design constituency*) is a precisely defined subset of end-users, stakeholders, or beneficiaries for whom a particular product, service, system, or experience is being intentionally designed, engineered, and optimized.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework** for *Design Process and Perspective*, a Target Group is not merely a passive "customer profile." It is an **active design variable** — a foundational input that constrains the entire downstream design space, including functional requirements, aesthetic decisions, ergonomic specifications, usability heuristics, accessibility parameters, and the validation criteria used during user testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Target Group identification is positioned as **Module 2's pivotal transitional concept** — it bridges *Design Theory* (Module 1) and the practical *Ideation / Prototyping* phases (Modules 3 & 4). Skipping rigorous target group analysis is the most common reason KTU examiners deduct marks in design case-study questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tailor's Fitting Room"

Imagine you walk into a master tailor's shop. Before the tailor cuts a single piece of fabric, they ask you three questions:
1. **Who will wear this garment?** (A 6-foot athletic male? A petite elderly woman? A child?)
2. **Where and when will it be worn?** (Office in summer? Wedding in winter? Marathon running?)
3. **What constraints matter?** (Budget? Mobility? Climate? Cultural modesty?)

The tailor does **not** design "a shirt." The tailor designs *a shirt for THIS specific person under THESE specific conditions.* That specific person — that demographic, psychographic, and behavioral profile — is the **Target Group**.

In design engineering:
- The **garment** = the product, service, or system being created.
- The **fit, fabric, and finish** = the design specifications.
- The **person** = the **Target Group**.

If the tailor misidentifies the person, even an exquisitely stitched shirt will be unworn.

## 1.3 Why Target Groups Matter in KTU Design Pedagogy

| Aspect | Without Target Group | With Target Group |
|---|---|---|
| Design Decisions | Vague, "one-size-fits-all" | Focused, context-driven |
| Validation | Subjective ("I think users will like it") | Empirical (tested against defined personas) |
| Resource Allocation | Scattered, inefficient | Prioritized, ROI-driven |
| KTU Exam Answer Quality | Generic, low marks | Specific, high marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box (Board Exam Favorites):**
> - **Primary Target Group** — The direct, intended end-user of the design outcome.
> - **Secondary Target Group** — Influencers, decision-makers, or caretakers (e.g., parents buying for children, doctors prescribing devices).
> - **Tertiary Target Group** — Indirect stakeholders affected by the design (e.g., maintenance staff, recyclers, bystanders).
> - **Persona** — A fictional, research-based archetype representing a target group member.
> - **Empathy Map** — A visual tool capturing what a target group *says, thinks, does, and feels*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 5-Layer Target Group Anatomy

A rigorous Target Group definition, as expected in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE answers, must articulate **five analytical layers**. A design answer that lists only one or two layers will lose marks under the *Course Outcome (CO2): "Analyse user contexts and stakeholder ecosystems"* assessment criterion.

### Layer 1 — Demographic Segmentation
Hard, quantifiable attributes of the user.
- Age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size, geography.

### Layer 2 — Psychographic Segmentation
Internal, attitudinal attributes.
- Values, beliefs, lifestyle, personality, aspirations, risk-tolerance.

### Layer 3 — Behavioral Segmentation
Observable usage patterns.
- Usage frequency, loyalty status, benefits sought, readiness-to-purchase, user journey stage.

### Layer 4 — Contextual / Environmental Segmentation
Situational constraints.
- Physical environment (climate, terrain), digital environment (device, bandwidth), social environment (cultural norms), temporal environment (time-of-day use).

### Layer 5 — Capability / Accessibility Segmentation
Inclusive-design parameters.
- Physical ability, cognitive ability, sensory ability, technical literacy, economic access.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" — Target Group Identification Matrix

Because this is a design-process course (not calculus), the "formulas" are **structured analytical templates**. KTU examiners award full marks when answers explicitly reference these structured frameworks.

| Framework / Tool | Purpose | When to Use in Design Process | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Demographic Pyramid** | Visualize age-gender distribution | Early scoping | Population pyramid chart |
| **Stakeholder Map** (Mendelow Matrix) | Plot power vs. interest | Module 2 analysis | 2×2 quadrant placement |
| **Empathy Map Canvas** | Capture user mindset | Module 2 synthesis | 4-quadrant canvas |
| **Persona Template** | Synthesize archetype | Module 2 documentation | 1-page persona card |
| **Jobs-To-Be-Done (JTBD)** | Define user "hired" need | Module 2–3 transition | Job statement + outcome |
| **User Journey Map** | Trace end-to-end experience | Module 3 validation | Stage-by-stage map |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mendelow's Power-Interest Matrix** (a frequently-tested KTU concept) classifies stakeholders into **four quadrants**:
> - *High Power + High Interest* → **Manage Closely** (Key Players / Primary Target)
> - *High Power + Low Interest* → **Keep Satisfied** (Secondary Target / Influencer)
> - *Low Power + High Interest* → **Keep Informed** (End-Users / Beneficiaries)
> - *Low Power + Low Interest* → **Monitor** (Tertiary / Peripheral)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional design practice — whether the domain is **biomedical device design**, **smart-city infrastructure**, **agricultural machinery**, or **educational technology** — Target Group analysis directly drives:

- **Specification engineering:** A wheelchair designed for a *spinal-cord-injury patient* (Layer 5) differs radically from one designed for an *elderly user with arthritis*.
- **Regulatory compliance:** Medical device target groups must align with **ISO 13485** and **FDA / CDSCO** intended-use populations.
- **Inclusive design compliance:** Aligns with the **UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD)** and India's *Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016*.
- **Sustainable design:** Target groups must include *end-of-life* stakeholders (recyclers, waste pickers) — this is now a **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned sustainability mandate**.

## 2.4 The KTU-Mandated Target Group Statement Template

When writing answers, KTU 2024 Scheme examiners expect target groups to be expressed using a **structured statement template** (worth 2 marks in any design question):

$$\text{Target Group Statement} = \underbrace{[\text{Demographic Archetype}]}_{\text{Layer 1}} + \underbrace{[\text{Psychographic Need}]}_{\text{Layer 2}} + \underbrace{[\text{Behavioral Context}]}_{\text{Layer 3}} + \underbrace{[\text{Capability Profile}]}_{\text{Layer 4-5}}$$

**Example:** *"Urban Indian middle-class women, aged 28–40, working full-time, with moderate digital literacy, who seek time-efficient home-cooking solutions and face daily time-constraint stress."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Target Group Identification Process

## 3.1 The 7-Step Target Group Derivation (Exhaustive, KTU-Marked)

Below is the **complete, non-truncated analytical derivation** of a Target Group from raw context to validated design brief. Each step is expanded with the logic a KTU examiner expects to see.

---

### STEP 1 — Capture the Design Brief
Document the **design problem statement**. What is being designed, and what problem does it solve?

> *"Design an affordable, portable water-purification device for rural Kerala."*

---

### STEP 2 — Broad User Universe Brainstorm
List **every possible human** who could conceivably interact with the design — directly, indirectly, or peripherally.

| # | Possible User | Direct / Indirect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rural household primary user (adult woman) | Direct |
| 2 | Children in household | Direct |
| 3 | Elderly in household | Direct |
| 4 | Local Panchayat health worker | Secondary |
| 5 | District Medical Officer | Influencer |
| 6 | Local vendor / distributor | Commercial |
| 7 | Maintenance technician | Operational |
| 8 | End-of-life recycler | Tertiary |

---

### STEP 3 — Apply Stakeholder Power-Interest Mapping (Mendelow Matrix)

Plot each user on the **Power × Interest** grid to prioritize. This step *mathematically* (visually) reduces the universe to the most consequential groups.

$$\text{Priority Score} = (\text{Power Weight} \times 0.5) + (\text{Interest Weight} \times 0.5)$$

Assigning values on a 1–5 scale:

| User | Power | Interest | Score | Quadrant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rural adult woman | 2 | 5 | 3.5 | Keep Informed |
| District Medical Officer | 5 | 4 | 4.5 | Manage Closely |
| Health worker | 4 | 5 | 4.5 | Manage Closely |
| Vendor | 3 | 3 | 3.0 | Keep Satisfied |
| Recycler | 1 | 2 | 1.5 | Monitor |

**Inference:** The *Manage Closely* quadrant contains the **Primary Target Group**. The KTU board expects you to *explicitly state this inference*.

---

### STEP 4 — Layer 1 Demographic Drill-Down
For the Primary Target Group (rural adult woman in Wayanad, Kerala):
- **Age range:** 25–55
- **Literacy:** Basic Malayalam reading; limited English
- **Income:** ₹8,000–₹18,000/month household
- **Family structure:** Joint family, 4–6 members
- **Geography:** Wayanad / Idukki hill-district villages

---

### STEP 5 — Layer 2 & 3 Psychographic + Behavioral Drill-Down
- **Values:** Strong community orientation; trust in local word-of-mouth.
- **Aspirations:** Safe drinking water for children's health without monthly disposable-income strain.
- **Behavior:** Currently uses boil-and-store method (time: 45 min/day); fetches water from communal pump.
- **Pain points:** Fuel-wood scarcity, time poverty, monsoon contamination spikes.

---

### STEP 6 — Layer 4 & 5 Contextual + Capability Drill-Down
- **Environment:** High humidity, monsoon flooding, intermittent electricity, limited repair-shop access.
- **Capability:** Moderate manual dexterity; no smartphone dependency for core function (but may use one for refill alerts).
- **Accessibility:** Device weight must be manageable on head-carry routes.

---

### STEP 7 — Synthesize the Final Target Group Statement

Applying the template from Section 2.4:

> *"Rural Kerala women, aged 25–55, primary household water-managers, with basic Malayalam literacy, limited disposable income (₹8,000–₹18,000/month household), operating in monsoon-prone hilly terrain with intermittent electricity, seeking affordable, low-maintenance, culturally-acceptable water purification."*

This **single-paragraph synthesized statement** is what a KTU top-scoring answer would present before proceeding to ideation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Persona Card (KTU Board-Standard Output)

A persona card is the **most commonly-tested visual deliverable** in KTU design-process questions. The template below represents the full credit-bearing version.

| Persona Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Name** | Latha Chechi |
| **Photo / Archetype** | [Visual placeholder — smiling woman in mundu] |
| **Age** | 38 |
| **Occupation** | Homemaker + part-time tapioca farmer |
| **Location** | Pulpally, Wayanad |
| **Income (Household)** | ₹12,000/month |
| **Education** | 8th standard |
| **Family** | Husband, 2 children (ages 8 & 12), mother-in-law |
| **Daily Routine (relevant excerpt)** | Fetches 40 L water from bore-well at 6 AM; boils for 30 min; stores in brass vessel |
| **Goals** | Ensure children drink clean water; reduce boil-time |
| **Frustrations** | Monsoon diarrhea episodes; rising LPG cost; fuel-wood scarcity |
| **Preferred Solution Channels** | Local Anganwadi worker, Kudumbashree meeting, word-of-mouth |
| **Quote (Voice of Customer)** | *"If something safe is there for less than a day's wage, I will try it."* |

> [!NOTE]
> The "Quote" field is **critical** — it provides *qualitative empathy evidence* and signals to the examiner that the student has moved beyond demographics into genuine user understanding.

---

## 3.3 Common Student Errors — Derivation Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Why It Loses Marks | KTU-Compliant Correction |
|---|---|---|
| Stopping at demographic only | Misses Layers 2–5 | Always complete all 5 layers |
| Vague target group ("users", "people") | Zero specificity | Use concrete arch-type with numbers |
| Confusing *target group* with *stakeholder* | Conceptual error | Target = end-user; Stakeholder = broader ecosystem |
| No evidence / no quote | Reads as assumption | Include Voice-of-Customer or field data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The 7-Step Target Group Identification Flow

This Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete derivation sequence** a KTU top-scoring answer would present.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Capture Design Brief] --> B[Step 2: Broad User Universe Brainstorm]
    B --> C[Step 3: Mendelow Power-Interest Mapping]
    C --> D[Step 4: Layer 1 Demographic Drill-Down]
    D --> E[Step 5: Layer 2 and 3 Psychographic plus Behavioral Drill-Down]
    E --> F[Step 6: Layer 4 and 5 Contextual plus Capability Drill-Down]
    F --> G[Step 7: Synthesize Final Target Group Statement]
    G --> H[Deliverable: Persona Card plus Empathy Map]
    H --> I[Validation: Field Interviews plus Iterative Refinement]
    I --> J[Output: Approved Target Group Profile for Module 3 Ideation]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Mendelow's Power-Interest Stakeholder Matrix

A 2×2 quadrant visualization — a **KTU 2024 high-frequency exam diagram**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QUAD_HIGH_POWER_HIGH_INTEREST["QUADRANT 1: MANAGE CLOSELY"]
        Q1A["District Medical Officer"]
        Q1B["Local ASHA Worker"]
        Q1C["Panchayat President"]
    end

    subgraph QUAD_HIGH_POWER_LOW_INTEREST["QUADRANT 2: KEEP SATISFIED"]
        Q2A["State Health Ministry"]
        Q2B["District Collector"]
    end

    subgraph QUAD_LOW_POWER_HIGH_INTEREST["QUADRANT 3: KEEP INFORMED"]
        Q3A["Rural Women End Users"]
        Q3B["Children in Households"]
        Q3C["Elderly Caregivers"]
    end

    subgraph QUAD_LOW_POWER_LOW_INTEREST["QUADRANT 4: MONITOR"]
        Q4A["Maintenance Vendors"]
        Q4B["End-of-Life Recyclers"]
        Q4C["Local Media"]
    end

    QUAD_HIGH_POWER_LOW_INTEREST --- QUAD_HIGH_POWER_HIGH_INTEREST
    QUAD_LOW_POWER_HIGH_INTEREST --- QUAD_LOW_POWER_LOW_INTEREST

    style QUAD_HIGH_POWER_HIGH_INTEREST fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    style QUAD_HIGH_POWER_LOW_INTEREST fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    style QUAD_LOW_POWER_HIGH_INTEREST fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style QUAD_LOW_POWER_LOW_INTEREST fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#616161,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the matrix:** Primary Target Group candidates are concentrated in Quadrant 1 (red) and Quadrant 3 (green). Quadrant 3 typically contains the *direct end-users* — even with low power, their high interest makes them central to design decisions.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Empathy Map (4-Quadrant User-Mindset Canvas)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAYS["SAYS — What the user says out loud"]
        S1["I want clean water for my children"]
        S2["Boiling takes too much time"]
        S3["Cost is a big worry"]
    end

    subgraph THINKS["THINKS — Internal thoughts the user keeps private"]
        T1["Will this really work?"]
        T2["What if the neighbours laugh at me?"]
        T3["Maybe the Anganwadi teacher knows more"]
    end

    subgraph DOES["DOES — Observable actions and behaviours"]
        D1["Fetches water at 6 AM daily"]
        D2["Boils water for 30 minutes on LPG"]
        D3["Stores in brass vessel with cloth cover"]
    end

    subgraph FEELS["FEELS — Emotional state and gut reactions"]
        F1["Anxious during monsoon about child sickness"]
        F2["Proud when family stays healthy"]
        F3["Frustrated by fuel costs"]
    end

    CENTER["Latha Chechi — Rural Wayanad Homemaker"] --- SAYS
    CENTER --- THINKS
    CENTER --- DOES
    CENTER --- FEELS

    style CENTER fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style SAYS fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style THINKS fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    style DOES fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style FEELS fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Functional Architecture: Target Group → Design Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[INPUT: Target Group Profile]
    PROCESS1[Process: Empathy Mapping]
    PROCESS2[Process: Persona Synthesis]
    PROCESS3[Process: JTBD Formulation]
    PROCESS4[Process: Constraint Extraction]

    OUTPUT1[OUTPUT: Design Brief]
    OUTPUT2[OUTPUT: Specification Document]
    OUTPUT3[OUTPUT: Validation Criteria]
    OUTPUT4[OUTPUT: Module 3 Ideation Input]

    INPUT --> PROCESS1 --> PROCESS2
    PROCESS2 --> PROCESS3 --> PROCESS4
    PROCESS4 --> OUTPUT1
    PROCESS4 --> OUTPUT2
    PROCESS4 --> OUTPUT3
    PROCESS4 --> OUTPUT4

    style INPUT fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style OUTPUT4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Target Group" as used in the design process. Differentiate between Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary target groups with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Target Group** is a precisely defined subset of end-users or beneficiaries for whom a specific product, service, or system is designed. (1 mark)
>
> - **Primary Target Group:** The direct end-user who operates or consumes the design. *Example:* For a digital blood-pressure monitor for home use, the **patient / caregiver at home** is the primary target. (1 mark)
> - **Secondary Target Group:** Influencers, decision-makers, or facilitators who enable purchase or adoption. *Example:* The **prescribing physician** who recommends the monitor. (0.5 mark)
> - **Tertiary Target Group:** Indirect stakeholders affected by the design lifecycle. *Example:* The **biomedical-waste recycler** who handles end-of-life disposal. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three segmentation criteria used to define a target group. Briefly explain the Empathy Map tool.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Three segmentation criteria:** (1.5 marks — 0.5 each)
> 1. **Demographic** — age, income, education, geography.
> 2. **Psychographic** — values, lifestyle, attitudes, aspirations.
> 3. **Behavioral** — usage frequency, loyalty, benefits sought.
>
> **Empathy Map** is a 4-quadrant visual tool that captures a target-group member's mindset by recording what they **SAY** (verbal expressions), **THINK** (private thoughts), **DO** (observable actions), and **FEEL** (emotions). (1.5 marks) It enables designers to move beyond surface demographics into genuine user empathy, ensuring design decisions are grounded in lived experience.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question (either OR). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A design team is tasked with creating a low-cost, solar-powered irrigation controller for smallholder farmers in Palakkad district, Kerala. Apply the complete Target Group identification methodology.**

**(a)** Identify and justify the **Primary Target Group** using Mendelow's Power-Interest Matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **Persona Card** and an **Empathy Map** for the identified Primary Target Group. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Universe of Users (1 mark)**

Possible users include:
- Smallholder farmer (owner-operator)
- Farmer's spouse (decision-influencer on cost)
- Agricultural Officer (extension advice)
- Local Krishi Bhavan staff
- Solar-panel installer / maintenance vendor
- Neighbouring farmers (peer-influence network)
- End-of-life equipment recycler

**Step 2: Mendelow Mapping with Justification (3 marks)**

| Stakeholder | Power (1-5) | Interest (1-5) | Score | Quadrant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smallholder farmer | 3 | 5 | 4.0 | Keep Informed → Manage Closely |
| Spouse | 2 | 4 | 3.0 | Keep Informed |
| Agricultural Officer | 5 | 4 | 4.5 | Manage Closely |
| Krishi Bhavan Staff | 4 | 3 | 3.5 | Keep Satisfied |
| Maintenance Vendor | 2 | 2 | 2.0 | Monitor |
| Peer farmers | 2 | 4 | 3.0 | Keep Informed |

**Justification:** The smallholder farmer is the **Primary Target Group** because (i) he/she is the direct operator and beneficiary of irrigation; (ii) purchase decision rests on his/her conviction; (iii) usage behaviour must be designed for his/her daily reality. The Agricultural Officer is a secondary influence but not the operator.

**Step 3: Final Primary Target Group Statement (3 marks)**

> *"Smallholder farmers in Palakkad, Kerala, aged 35–60, owning 0.5–2 acres of paddy or vegetable farmland, with monthly household income ₹15,000–₹30,000, limited formal agricultural training but high traditional-knowledge literacy, exposed to erratic monsoon and canal-water availability, seeking affordable solar-powered automation to reduce manual valve-operation labour."*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Persona Card (4 marks):**

| Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| Name | Raviettan |
| Age | 47 |
| Location | Pattambi, Palakkad |
| Land Holding | 1.5 acres paddy |
| Income | ₹22,000/month (seasonal variation) |
| Education | 10th standard |
| Family | Wife + 2 school-going children |
| Current Practice | Manually opens field sluice gates at 5 AM and 5 PM; loses 2 hours daily |
| Goal | Reduce manual labour; irrigate even when away for market days |
| Frustration | Erratic monsoons; pump fuel cost; voltage fluctuations damaging existing electric pump |
| Voice of Customer | *"If the pump starts by itself when water is low, I can spend the saved time on my second job."* |

**Empathy Map (3 marks):**

- **Says:** *"Irrigation is the most tiring part of my day."* *"Solar is good, but is it affordable?"*
- **Thinks:** *"Will it work in cloudy monsoon weeks?"* *"My neighbour Krishnan tried drip and it failed."*
- **Does:** Walks to field twice daily to operate valve; relies on agricultural-office SMS advisories; consults village cooperative leader before purchases.
- **Feels:** Anxious during dry spells; proud as self-reliant farmer; sceptical of "new technology" promises after past failures.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A multidisciplinary student team is designing a mobile-based mental-health screening and counselling referral app for college students across Kerala.**

**(a)** Define the **Primary Target Group** using a 5-Layer Target Group Anatomy (demographic, psychographic, behavioral, contextual, capability). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the **Stakeholder Map** and **Jobs-To-Be-Done (JTBD) framework** together refine the target-group definition for this design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

| Layer | Specification for Primary Target Group |
|---|---|
| **1. Demographic** (1.5) | Kerala college students aged 18–24, enrolled in undergraduate programmes across government and private colleges, mixed gender, urban / semi-urban, family income ₹20,000–₹80,000/month, Malayalam-medium digital literacy |
| **2. Psychographic** (1.5) | High academic-pressure exposure; anxiety-prone personality cluster prevalent; strong peer-influence orientation; growing mental-health awareness but persisting stigma; preference for anonymous, asynchronous help-seeking |
| **3. Behavioral** (1) | Daily smartphone usage 4–6 hours; active on Instagram, WhatsApp; has consulted Google / YouTube for mental-health queries; rarely visits campus counsellor due to visibility concerns |
| **4. Contextual** (1.5) | Hostel / shared-room living (privacy-constrained); intermittent Wi-Fi in college; high academic-deadline stress windows (exams, placements); family-privacy norms discourage home-based sessions |
| **5. Capability** (1.5) | High smartphone literacy; moderate English; comfortable with Malayalam text; mild visual / auditory accessibility needs (a small but design-critical sub-segment) |

**Final Target Group Statement:**

> *"Kerala undergraduate students, aged 18–24, smartphone-native, experiencing academic and social-pressure-driven anxiety, preferring anonymous, low-friction, Malayalam-friendly digital mental-health support, constrained by hostel privacy and stigma-related concerns."*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Stakeholder Map Application (3.5 marks):**

The team must map *all* stakeholders on the Power-Interest grid:

- **Manage Closely (High-High):** Students themselves, college counsellors, District Mental Health Programme officer.
- **Keep Satisfied (High-Low):** College Principal, University Vice-Chancellor, parents (high power, lower direct interest).
- **Keep Informed (Low-High):** Peer student-counsellors, campus wellness clubs, NGO partners.
- **Monitor (Low-Low):** App-store moderation teams, telecom data-handling partners.

**Insight:** Mapping reveals that *while parents hold high power, their lower direct interest means the design must NOT be parent-gated* — the app's anonymous-first architecture is justified by this stakeholder reality.

**JTBD Framework Application (3.5 marks):**

For each stakeholder segment, articulate the *"hired job"*:

$$\text{JTBD Statement} = \text{When } [\text{situation}], \text{ I want to } [\text{motivation}], \text{ so I can } [\text{outcome}].$$

- **Student:** *"When exam-stress spikes at 2 AM and I cannot sleep, I want to take a confidential self-assessment, so I can decide whether to seek professional help without anyone knowing yet."*
- **College Counsellor:** *"When a student is flagged at-risk by the app, I want to receive a structured referral with consent, so I can intervene early during my available hours."*
- **District Officer:** *"When monthly app data aggregates are available, I want anonymized dashboards, so I can allocate counsellor resources district-wide."*

**Synthesis:** The combination of Stakeholder Map + JTBD transforms the abstract target group into **three role-specific design briefs** — primary user, secondary service-provider, tertiary institutional buyer — each with distinct success criteria for Module 3 ideation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Target Group questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing target groups with stakeholders** (–2 marks). Target Group = end-user; Stakeholder = broader ecosystem.
> 2. **Single-layer answers** (–3 marks). Listing only demographics ("rural women aged 25–55") without psychographic, behavioral, contextual, and capability layers signals superficial understanding.
> 3. **No persona or empathy-map deliverable** (–2 marks). KTU 2024 Scheme expects *visual / structured artifacts* in any 14-mark Target Group question.
> 4. **Generic empathy map** (–1 mark). Saying "users feel happy" is insufficient. The map must be **specific to the design context** ("Latha Chechi feels anxious during monsoon about her children's diarrhea").
> 5. **No Voice-of-Customer quote** (–1 mark). Always include a fictional-but-plausible user quote to demonstrate empathy immersion.
> 6. **Skipping stakeholder-mapping rationale** (–1 mark). Merely listing quadrants without explaining *why* a stakeholder is in that quadrant loses credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Target Groups (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Target Group = precisely-defined end-user subset for whom a design is intentionally created.
- ☐ **Three Categories:** Primary (direct user), Secondary (influencer / decision-maker), Tertiary (indirect lifecycle stakeholder).
- ☐ **Five-Layer Anatomy:** Demographic → Psychographic → Behavioral → Contextual → Capability.
- ☐ **Mendelow Matrix:** Power × Interest = Manage Closely / Keep Satisfied / Keep Informed / Monitor.
- ☐ **Empathy Map Quadrants:** SAYS / THINKS / DOES / FEELS — must be context-specific.
- ☐ **Persona Card Fields:** Name, demographics, goals, frustrations, voice-of-customer quote.
- ☐ **JTBD Template:** *When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome].*
- ☐ **Template for Target Group Statement:** *Demographic + Psychographic + Behavioral + Capability*.
- ☐ **Common Pitfall:** Single-layer demographic-only answers lose 3+ marks.
- ☐ **Mandatory Deliverables:** Persona Card + Empathy Map + Stakeholder Map for any 14-mark answer.
- ☐ **Inclusivity:** Always consider accessibility Layer-5 stakeholders (per UNCRPD and KTU NEP 2020 alignment).
- ☐ **Sustainability:** Include end-of-life recyclers as tertiary target group (NEP 2020 mandate).
- ☐ **Module 2 → Module 3 Bridge:** A well-defined Target Group is the *input* for Module 3 Ideation; without it, ideation becomes arbitrary and loses KTU marks.
- ☐ **Exam Strategy:** In any design question, *always start* by stating the target group explicitly — even if the question does not ask for it. This signals structured thinking and earns "process marks."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Idea Generation for the Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT637 - DESIGN PROCESS AND PERSPECTIVE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Idea Generation for the Problem # MODULE 2: IDEA GENERATION FOR THE PROBLEM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Idea Generation (Concept Generation)** is the **divergent phase** of the engineering design process in which a designer or design team systematically produces a **broad, unstructured, and quantity-oriented set of potential solutions** to a clearly defined design problem statement, *prior to* any filtering, evaluation, or selection. In formal design methodology (cross-referenced with Ulrich & Eppinger, Pahl & Beitz, and the KTU 2024 PECNT637 syllabus), idea generation is positioned **between** *Problem Definition / Customer Needs Analysis* (Module 1 territory) and *Concept Selection / Concept Screening* (Module 3 territory). It is the creative fulcrum of the entire design process. The mathematical/programmatic goal of this phase can be expressed as a search-space expansion: $$\text{Idea Set } \mathcal{I} = \{I_1, I_2, I_3, \dots, I_n\} \quad \text{where} \quad n \to \text{maximize diversity, not quality}$$ The **metric of success** at this stage is **breadth and variety**, NOT the quality of any single idea. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Mango Tree" Metaphor** > > Imagine you are hungry and need food. **Problem Definition** is recognizing "I am hungry." **Idea Generation** is suddenly shouting out everything you can think of: *"Idli! Dosa! Biryani! Pizza! Mango! Leftover rice!"* — even wild or impractical ideas like *"I could eat a rock"* or *"I could photosynthesize."* You do NOT judge them yet. You just collect as many as possible. > > **Concept Selection** (next module) is when you later sit down, filter, and pick "Pizza" because it fits your time, budget, and taste. > > **The key insight:** If you only generated "Idli" and "Dosa," you might miss the best answer ("Pizza"). The MORE diverse ideas you generate, the HIGHER your chance of finding the optimal solution. ### 1.3 Position in the KTU Design Process Flow The KTU 2024 syllabus places Idea Generation as the **second major phase** of the design process, immediately following Problem Identification and preceding Concept Selection: $$\text{Problem Identified} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Idea Generation}} \rightarrow \text{Concept Selection} \rightarrow \text{Prototype} \rightarrow \text{Testing}$$ ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Idea Generation The following quantitative and qualitative metrics are tracked during this phase (highlighted in **bold** for KTU exam recall): - **Quantity (N)** — Total number of ideas generated. KTU standard expectation: minimum **20–50 ideas per problem** in a typical 1-hour student brainstorming session. - **Diversity Score** — Spread across functional, structural, and behavioral solution categories. - **Novelty Index** — Percentage of ideas that are *non-obvious* or *out-of-category*. - **Participation Rate** — Number of active contributors vs. silent members in a team setting. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The official PECNT637 syllabus specifically mandates exposure to **at least 5 distinct idea generation techniques**, ensuring students can adaptively choose the right method based on problem type, team size, and time constraints. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Divergent vs. Convergent Thinking Idea Generation is rooted in **divergent thinking** — the cognitive process of generating multiple creative ideas by exploring many possible solutions. This was formally distinguished from **convergent thinking** by psychologist J.P. Guilford (1950) and later popularized by Edward de Bono. | Cognitive Mode | Purpose | Direction | Evaluation | When Used | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Divergent Thinking** | Generate many options | Outward / Expanding | Postponed | Idea Generation | | **Convergent Thinking** | Pick the best option | Inward / Narrowing | Immediate | Concept Selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often *converge too early* — judging ideas during brainstorming. This is the **#1 violation** of the idea generation phase and the most commonly penalized error in KTU valuation. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Idea Generation The phase is governed by a strict **6-step procedural logic**: 1. **Reaffirm the Problem Statement** — Re-read the design brief; pin it visibly on the wall. 2. **Set Generation Rules** — *No criticism*, *quantity over quality*, *wild ideas welcome*, *combine & build on others' ideas*. 3. **Stimulus Activation** — Use a trigger (image, word, analogy, failure mode, customer quote) to seed thoughts. 4. **Idea Eruption** — Rapid, timed output of ideas (typically 5–15 minute sprints). 5. **Capture & Document** — Write/record every idea verbatim; even "bad" ones. 6. **Defer Judgment** — All filtering is postponed to the next module (Concept Selection). ## 2.3 Comprehensive Idea Generation Techniques (KTU 2024 Mandated Set) ### 2.3.1 Brainstorming (Osborn, 1953) The original and most widely used technique. **Alex Osborn** formalized it in his book *Applied Imagination*. It relies on a group setting where free association is encouraged under four cardinal rules: - **Rule 1 — No Criticism:** Defer all judgment. - **Rule 2 — Quantity Wanted:** Generate as many as possible. - **Rule 3 — Freewheeling Encouraged:** Wild ideas are welcome. - **Rule 4 — Combinations & Improvements:** Build on others' ideas. ### 2.3.2 SCAMPER Technique (Bob Eberle) A structured checklist of **seven verbal triggers**, each prompting a different cognitive transformation on an existing product or concept: | Trigger Verb | Cognitive Operation | Example (Pen Redesign) | |---|---|---| | **S** — Substitute | Replace a component | Use bamboo instead of plastic barrel | | **C** — Combine | Merge two functions | Pen + USB drive | | **A** — Adapt | Adjust for new context | Adjustable-tip pen for artists | | **M** — Modify / Magnify | Change size, shape, color | Giant pen for signage | | **P** — Put to other use | Repurpose the object | Pen as a stylus | | **E** — Eliminate | Remove a part | Capless retractable design | | **R** — Reverse / Rearrange | Flip the order or orientation | Tip-up writing instrument | ### 2.3.3 Morphological Analysis (Fritz Zwicky, 1969) A systematic method that breaks the problem into **independent functional parameters** and generates combinations by mixing alternatives for each parameter. $$\text{Parameter}_1 \times \text{Parameter}_2 \times \dots \times \text{Parameter}_k = \text{Total Combinatorial Space } \mathcal{C}$$ If each parameter has $n_i$ alternatives, the total search space is: $$\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$$ For example, a coffee mug design might have 3 parameters (Material × Lid Type × Handle Shape) with 3 options each, generating $3^3 = 27$ distinct combinations to evaluate. ### 2.3.4 Synectics (William Gordon, 1961) Uses **analogies and metaphors** to force the mind out of conventional patterns. Four types of analogies are used: - **Direct Analogy** — Compare to a similar function in nature (e.g., a fan blade ↔ a bird wing). - **Personal Analogy** — Empathize: *"What if I were the fan blade? How would I feel?"* - **Fantasy Analogy** — Imagine a magical, physics-violating solution. - **Symbolic Analogy** — Compress the problem into a poetic symbol or image. ### 2.3.5 TRIZ (Genrich Altshuller, 1946) **T**eoriya **R**esheniya **I**zobretatelskikh **Z**adatch — "Theory of Inventive Problem Solving." Based on the analysis of **2 million patents**, Altshuller identified **40 Inventive Principles** and **39 Engineering Parameters** that map contradictions to standardized solutions. Particularly useful for **engineering-heavy** problems. ### 2.3.6 Mind Mapping (Tony Buzan, 1974) A non-linear, radial visual technique where a central problem statement is surrounded by branches of ideas, sub-ideas, and associations. Particularly effective for **individual ideation** rather than group sessions. ### 2.3.7 Biomimicry (Janine Benyus, 1997) Solution patterns are derived by **mimicking biological strategies** observed in nature. Examples: - **Velcro** ↔ Burdock plant burrs (George de Mestral, 1955). - **Bullet train nose** ↔ Kingfisher beak (Eiji Nakatsu). - **Sharkskin surface** ↔ Riblet pattern for drag reduction. ### 2.3.8 6 Thinking Hats (Edward de Bono, 1985) A **parallel thinking** framework where team members deliberately adopt six distinct cognitive roles (each represented by a colored hat) to explore the problem from all angles. Used to **structure** rather than restrict idea generation. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Technique | Inventor | Year | Group / Individual | Best For | Time Required | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Brainstorming | Alex Osborn | 1953 | Group | General ideation, broad problems | 30–60 min | | SCAMPER | Bob Eberle | 1971 | Either | Improving existing products | 20–40 min | | Morphological Analysis | Fritz Zwicky | 1969 | Either | Combinatorial design spaces | 45–90 min | | Synectics | William Gordon | 1961 | Group | Stalled problems needing fresh angles | 60+ min | | TRIZ | Genrich Altshuller | 1946 | Individual | Engineering contradictions | 1–3 hrs | | Mind Mapping | Tony Buzan | 1974 | Individual | Initial exploration, unstructured problems | 15–30 min | | Biomimicry | Janine Benyus | 1997 | Either | Sustainable / nature-aligned design | 1–2 hrs | | 6 Thinking Hats | Edward de Bono | 1985 | Group | Multi-perspective evaluation | 45–75 min | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Heuristic:** When asked *"Which technique is best for [X]?"* in the exam, the model answer is rarely a single technique. Top-scoring answers justify the choice with **two layers**: (1) why the technique matches the *problem type*, and (2) why alternatives were rejected. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Idea generation is the phase where intellectual property is **born**. Every engineered product — from the iPhone to the Airbus A380 — emerged from a divergent exploration of hundreds of ideas before being narrowed to one. In professional practice, idea generation directly determines: - **Patent portfolio breadth** for technology companies. - **Time-to-market** — broader initial exploration reduces costly late-stage rework. - **Innovation index** of R&D departments (a measurable KPI in industry). - **Risk diversification** — multiple concepts hedge against single-concept failure. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic also feeds into **Project-Based Learning (PBL)** and **Capstone Design Projects**, where students are expected to generate and document a minimum number of solutions before prototype fabrication. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Comparative Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Procedural Derivation: The Idea Generation Workflow Below is the **fully expanded procedural chain** — every transition justified, every decision rule made explicit. There are **no skipped steps**. ### Step 1 — Reaffirmation of the Design Brief $$\text{Brief} = \{ \text{Needs}, \text{Constraints}, \text{Success Criteria} \}$$ The student must visibly restate the problem. This step *prevents drift* during the high-energy creative phase. If the team wanders, they re-read the brief. > **Logic:** Without this anchor, divergent thinking becomes *divergent from the actual problem*, not from the solution space. ### Step 2 — Activation of Prior Knowledge $$\text{Knowledge Pool} = \text{Personal Experience} \cup \text{Domain Research} \cup \text{Prior Art}$$ Review existing solutions, competitor products, related patents, and scientific principles. This **seeds** the associative network in the brain needed for creative combinations. ### Step 3 — Selection of an Appropriate Technique $$\text{Technique}^* = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \left[ \text{Fit}(T, \text{Problem Type}) + \text{Fit}(T, \text{Team Size}) + \text{Fit}(T, \text{Time Budget}) \right]$$ Where $\mathcal{T} = \{ \text{Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Morphological, Synectics, TRIZ, Mind Map, Biomimicry, 6 Hats} \}$. ### Step 4 — Setting of Operational Rules The four Osborn rules are made explicit and written on a visible board: $$\text{Rules} = \{ \text{No Criticism}, \text{Quality Last}, \text{Wild Welcome}, \text{Combine Always} \}$$ ### Step 5 — Timed Idea Eruption A strict timer (typically **5–15 minutes per round**) is imposed. The cognitive pressure of the timer suppresses the natural human tendency to self-censor. $$\text{Idea Count} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Time per Idea}} \quad \text{(inversely related, so shorten time to increase count)}$$ ### Step 6 — Capture Without Editing Every idea is captured *verbatim* on sticky notes, whiteboards, or digital boards. Even ideas deemed "useless" are recorded — they may be the seed of a better idea during combination. ### Step 7 — Combination & Building After the eruption phase, a **second pass** explicitly hunts for *combinations*: $$I_{\text{combined}} = I_a \oplus I_b \quad \text{for all } a, b \text{ in the idea set}$$ This is often where the *most innovative* ideas emerge — two mediocre ideas fused can yield one brilliant one. ### Step 8 — Closure & Documentation The session closes with a count, a categorization, and a hand-off package to the next phase (Concept Selection). ##
