| **Control Complexity** | 1–3 inputs (tap/swipe) | 12–16 inputs (gamepad) |
| **Demographic Breadth** | **Age 8 – 75**, unisex | **Age 12 – 45**, skewed male (historically) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Casual Game Design Architecture

Casual games are built on the principle of **"Frictionless Entry, Frictionless Exit."** The design philosophy is rooted in behavioral psychology — specifically **B.J. Fogg's Persuasive Technology Model** and **Nir Eyal's Hook Model**.

#### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars of Casual Design

1. **Immediate Gratification Loop**
   - Every action must produce visible feedback within **under 200 milliseconds**.
   - The reward variable (XP, coins, stars) must be **instantly legible**.

2. **Asynchronous Multiplayer (Light Social)**
   - No real-time dependency on other players.
   - Use of leaderboards, "ghost" data, and gifting mechanics.
   - Example: Sending lives in *Candy Crush Saga* via Facebook integration.

3. **Vertical Simplicity / Horizontal Depth**
   - Few mechanics, but each mechanic has many permutations.
   - Example: In *Angry Birds*, the single mechanic is "projectile trajectory," but the depth comes from physics variation per level.

4. **Episodic Content Delivery**
   - Content is shipped in **15-level chunks every 2–4 weeks** to maintain Daily Active Users (DAU).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When examiners ask "Why are casual games successful?", the four pillars above are the **exam-ready answer**. Memorize the verbs: *Immediate, Asynchronous, Vertical, Episodic*.

---

### 2.2 The Console Game Design Architecture

Console games follow the principle of **"Mastery Through Commitment."** The theoretical foundation rests on **Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory** and **Self-Determination Theory (SDT)** by Ryan & Deci.

#### 2.2.1 The Five Pillars of Console Design

1. **Synchronous Multiplayer (Heavy Social)**
   - Real-time co-op, PvP, raid mechanics.
   - Example: 4-player co-op in *It Takes Two*, 100-player lobbies in *Call of Duty*.

2. **Cinematic Narrative Arc**
   - Linear or branching storylines spanning **15–60 hours**.
   - Scripted set-pieces, motion-captured performances, orchestral scores.

3. **Progressive Mastery Curve**
   - Skills compound over time.
   - Example: Combos in *Devil May Cry*, build crafting in *Elden Ring*.

4. **Hardware-Exclusive Optimization**
   - Designed for fixed hardware (e.g., PS5's custom SSD reduces load times from 30s to **< 2 seconds**).
   - Leverages proprietary features (haptic feedback on DualSense, VR on PSVR2).

5. **Premium / Subscription Monetization**
   - One-time purchase (**$60–$70**), DLC packs, season passes, or subscription services (PS Plus, Game Pass).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **core analytical formulas** used to evaluate and design casual vs. console games. These are testable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| Symbol | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_{1}$ (Day-1 Retention) | $\dfrac{N_{d1}}{N_{install}} \times 100$ | % of users returning **24 hours** after install | Casual: **35–45%**, Console: N/A |
| $D_{7}$ (Day-7 Retention) | $\dfrac{N_{d7}}{N_{install}} \times 100$ | % of users returning **7 days** after install | Casual: **12–18%** |
| $ARPDAU$ | $\dfrac{\text{Total Daily Revenue}}{DAU}$ | Average Revenue Per Daily Active User | Casual: **\$0.05–\$0.30** |
| $LTV$ | $ARPDAU \times \text{Lifetime Days}$ | Lifetime Value of a user | Casual LTV: **\$1–\$10** |
| $CPI$ | $\dfrac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Installs}}$ | Cost Per Install | Casual: **\$1.5–\$5** |
| $K_{factor}$ | $i \times \dfrac{c}{100}$ | Viral coefficient ($i$ = invites, $c$ = conversion %) | Casual goal: $K > 1$ |
| $F_{score}$ | $\dfrac{\text{Tutorial Skip Rate}}{\text{Tutorial Length (min)}}$ | Friction Index of onboarding | Casual target: $F < 0.1$ |
| $S_{ideal}$ | $\dfrac{M_{core}}{\bar{t}_{session}}$ | Sessions per day from core loop | Casual: **3–8 sessions/day** |
| $B_{complex}$ | $\log_{2}(R_{mechanic})$ | Bit-depth of mechanical complexity | Console: $B > 12$ bits |
| $E_{ratio}$ | $\dfrac{E_{player}}{E_{max\text{-}possible}}$ | Engagement ratio (HUD telemetry) | Console: $E > 0.6$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value operations use the `\vert` LaTeX command instead of vertical pipes to preserve markdown table integrity. Example: $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The Casual vs. Console framework is not limited to entertainment. It is directly applied in:

- **UX Design** — distinguishing between micro-interactions (casual) and dashboard workflows (console).
- **Automotive HMI** — Tesla's touchscreen UI vs. BMW's iDrive controller.
- **Medical Device Design** — patient-facing apps (casual) vs. surgeon consoles (Da Vinci robot).
- **Education Technology** — Duolingo (casual) vs. Unity Learn (console-tier depth).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Casual Engagement Decay Function

We model how a player's engagement decays over time in a casual game. The decay follows an **exponential half-life model**, where each session reduces residual motivation by a constant ratio.

#### Step 1: State the Core Assumption
A player's engagement $E(t)$ at time $t$ follows first-order decay kinetics, analogous to radioactive decay.

#### Step 2: Write the Differential Equation
The rate of engagement loss is proportional to current engagement.

$$\dfrac{dE(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot E(t)$$

Where $\lambda$ is the decay constant (unit: **per day**), reflecting how quickly a player loses interest.

#### Step 3: Separate Variables and Integrate
Rearrange to isolate $E(t)$ and integrate from $E_{0}$ at $t=0$ to $E(t)$ at time $t$.

$$\int_{E_{0}}^{E(t)} \dfrac{dE}{E} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

#### Step 4: Evaluate the Integral
The natural logarithm emerges from the left integral.

$$\ln(E(t)) - \ln(E_{0}) = -\lambda t$$

#### Step 5: Exponentiate to Solve
Applying the exponential function $e^{x}$ to both sides yields the closed-form solution.

$$E(t) = E_{0} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

#### Step 6: Interpret the Half-Life
The half-life $t_{1/2}$ is defined as the time when $E(t) = \dfrac{E_{0}}{2}$. Solve by substitution.

$$\dfrac{E_{0}}{2} = E_{0} \cdot e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides to isolate the half-life.

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$-\ln(2) = -\lambda \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

#### Step 7: Apply to Casual vs. Console Context

- For **casual games**: $\lambda_{casual} \approx 0.07$ per day → $t_{1/2} \approx 10$ days. Hence the **episodic content** cadence.
- For **console games**: $\lambda_{console} \approx 0.005$ per day → $t_{1/2} \approx 138$ days. Hence the **single-launch blockbuster** model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Console Flow Channel Width

Based on **Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory**, the optimal engagement zone lies between anxiety (too hard) and boredom (too easy). We derive the width of this channel for console games.

#### Step 1: Define Skill and Challenge as Functions
Player skill grows logarithmically over time.

$$S(t) = S_{0} + \alpha \cdot \ln(1 + t)$$

Challenge difficulty is designer-controlled and grows linearly or exponentially.

$$C(t) = C_{0} \cdot \beta^{t}$$

#### Step 2: Define the Flow Condition
Flow occurs when skill and challenge are within a tolerance band $\Delta$.

$$\vert S(t) - C(t) \vert \leq \Delta$$

#### Step 3: Determine the Channel Width
The flow channel exists for all $t$ satisfying the inequality. For balanced design, the skill and challenge curves should be tangent at the optimal point $t^{*}$.

$$S'(t^{*}) = C'(t^{*})$$

Differentiate both expressions.

$$\dfrac{\alpha}{1 + t^{*}} = C_{0} \cdot \beta^{t^{*}} \cdot \ln(\beta)$$

#### Step 4: Solve for Optimal Time
The point $t^{*}$ is where the curves are perfectly matched.

$$t^{*} = \dfrac{\alpha}{C_{0} \cdot \beta^{t^{*}} \cdot \ln(\beta)} - 1$$

#### Step 5: Compute Channel Width
The total flow channel width $W$ is the integral of valid time intervals.

$$W = \int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} \mathbb{1}_{\vert S(t) - C(t) \vert \leq \Delta} \, dt$$

#### Step 6: Design Interpretation
- A **well-designed console game** has $W > 0$ over **at least 80% of the playtime**.
- A **well-designed casual game** has $W > 0$ over **at least 95% of any 5-minute session**, achieved by keeping the level discrete and self-contained.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The board expects you to know that **casual games optimize for short, wide flow channels per level**, while **console games optimize for long, deep flow channels across the campaign**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Design Vector | Casual Game | Console Game |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Psychology** | B.F. Skinner Variable Reinforcement | Csikszentmihalyi Flow Theory |
| **Failure Penalty** | None or "retry immediately" | Death, resource loss, save reload |
| **Session Save Model** | Cloud autosave every 5 seconds | Manual save points, autosaves at chapters |
| **UI Density** | Low (≤ 3 HUD elements) | High (minimap, health, ammo, objectives) |
| **Audio Loop Length** | 15–60 seconds | 4–8 minutes (dynamic adaptive scores) |
| **Tutorial Mode** | Embedded in first 30 seconds of play | Dedicated 10–20 minute tutorial level |
| **Community Vector** | Social media virality | Discord, Reddit, Twitch streaming |
| **Patch Cadence** | Weekly (forced updates) | Quarterly (optional patches) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Player Journey Comparison Flowchart

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the divergent player journey architectures for Casual and Console game genres.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([User Acquires Product]) --> nodeB{Platform Entry Point}
    nodeB -->|Mobile App Store| nodeC[Casual Pipeline]
    nodeB -->|Console Storefront| nodeD[Console Pipeline]

    nodeC --> nodeE[Install 5 Seconds]
    nodeE --> nodeF[First Tap Immediate]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Reward within 200ms]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Session Complete in 5 min}
    nodeH --> nodeI[Cloud Save Auto]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Push Notification in 24hr]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Return Loop Activated]

    nodeD --> nodeL[Download 50 GB]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Patch 5 GB Applied]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Tutorial 20 min]
    nodeN --> nodeO[First Story Beat]
    nodeO --> nodeP{Skill Checkpoint Hit}
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Mid Game 15 Hours]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Climax 40 Hours]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Credits Roll]
    nodeS --> nodeT[New Game Plus Unlocked]

    nodeK --> nodeU([Player Lifetime 90 Days])
    nodeT --> nodeV([Player Lifetime 3 Years])
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Choosing Casual vs. Console Design Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New Game Project]) --> q1{Target Session Length?}

    q1 -->|Under 15 Minutes| pathA[Casual Track]
    q1 -->|Over 30 Minutes| pathB[Console Track]

    pathA --> q2{Target Demographic?}
    q2 -->|Mass Market| nodeA1[Mobile First]
    q2 -->|Seniors and Casual| nodeA2[Tablet First]

    pathB --> q3{Story Priority?}
    q3 -->|Cinematic| nodeB1[Single Player AAA]
    q3 -->|Competitive| nodeB2[Multiplayer Service Game]

    nodeA1 --> outA([Revenue via Ads and IAP])
    nodeA2 --> outB([Revenue via Premium One Time])

    nodeB1 --> outC([Revenue via Box Sales and DLC])
    nodeB2 --> outD([Revenue via MTX and Season Pass])

    outA --> summary([Output Design Document])
    outB --> summary
    outC --> summary
    outD --> summary
```

---

### 4.3 Monetization Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CasualBlock[CASUAL REVENUE STACK]
        dirA[Free Download] --> dirB[Interstitial Ads]
        dirB --> dirC[Rewarded Video Ads]
        dirC --> dirD[In App Purchases]
        dirD --> dirE[Subscription Pass]
    end

    subgraph ConsoleBlock[CONSOLE REVENUE STACK]
        dirF[Premium Box Sale 70 USD] --> dirG[DLC Expansion 30 USD]
        dirG --> dirH[Season Pass 40 USD]
        dirH --> dirI[Microtransactions 5 USD]
        dirI --> dirJ[Subscription Service 15 USD per month]
    end

    CasualBlock --> metric[Combined ARPU Calculation]
    ConsoleBlock --> metric
    metric --> kpi([Key Performance Indicator Dashboard])
```

---

### 4.4 Engagement Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    loop1([Trigger External]) --> act1[Action Player Input]
    act1 --> reward1[Variable Reward]
    reward1 --> invest1[Investment of Time]
    invest1 --> loop1

    loop1 -.->|Console Variant| story[Story Branch Unlocked]
    story -.-> loop1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Casual Game" with two defining characteristics.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Casual Game is a digital entertainment product designed for **short play sessions of 3–15 minutes**, featuring **minimal learning curves** and **simple control inputs** (typically 1–3 actions like tap or swipe). Its two defining characteristics are: **(1) Frictionless Entry** — the player can begin gameplay within 60 seconds of installation without a tutorial; and **(2) Episodic Engagement** — content is delivered in bite-sized chunks (e.g., 15-level updates every 2 weeks) to maintain daily retention. **(1+1+1 = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous multiplayer as applied to console vs. casual games.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Synchronous multiplayer** requires all players to be online simultaneously in real time — this is the dominant model in **console games** (e.g., 100-player lobbies in *Fortnite*). **Asynchronous multiplayer** allows players to compete or collaborate without being online at the same time, using stored game state — this is the dominant model in **casual games** (e.g., ghost data in *Wordle* or leaderboards in *Subway Surfers*). The distinction arises because casual players have unpredictable availability, while console players typically schedule dedicated multiplayer sessions. **(1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks)**

---

### 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **four pillars of casual game design** with one real-world example each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four pillars are:

1. **Immediate Gratification Loop:** Every player action produces visible feedback within 200ms. *Example:* In *Angry Birds 2*, the moment a player releases the slingshot, the camera follows the bird and destruction particles spawn instantly.

2. **Asynchronous Multiplayer:** No real-time dependency on other players. *Example:* In *Clash Royale*, players attack opponents' bases using stored deck configurations, not live PvP.

3. **Vertical Simplicity / Horizontal Depth:** Few mechanics but many permutations. *Example:* In *2048*, the single swipe mechanic creates exponential complexity through number combinations.

4. **Episodic Content Delivery:** Content shipped in 2–4 week cycles. *Example:* *Pokémon GO* Community Days occur monthly with time-limited content.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition of each pillar (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks) + Real-world examples (1 × 4 = 4 marks) + Synthesis statement (1 mark) = **7 marks**.

---

**(b)** A casual game has the following telemetry: 1,000,000 installs on Day 0, 380,000 return on Day 1, and 145,000 return on Day 7. The total revenue over the first 30 days is **\$45,000**. Calculate:
- (i) **Day-1 Retention ($D_1$)**
- (ii) **Day-7 Retention ($D_7$)**
- (iii) **30-Day ARPDAU** assuming DAU = 150,000 **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Day-1 Retention:**

$$D_1 = \dfrac{N_{d1}}{N_{install}} \times 100$$

Substitute the given values.

$$D_1 = \dfrac{380{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100$$

$$D_1 = 38\%$$

This is within the healthy casual game range of **35–45%**. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Day-7 Retention:**

$$D_7 = \dfrac{N_{d7}}{N_{install}} \times 100$$

Substitute the given values.

$$D_7 = \dfrac{145{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100$$

$$D_7 = 14.5\%$$

This is within the acceptable casual game range of **12–18%**. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) 30-Day ARPDAU:**

$$ARPDAU = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{DAU}$$

Substitute the given values.

$$ARPDAU = \dfrac{45{,}000}{150{,}000}$$

$$ARPDAU = \$0.30 \text{ per DAU}$$

This is at the **high end** of the casual benchmark (\$0.05–\$0.30), indicating strong monetization performance. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **five pillars of console game design** and justify why each is necessary for the platform. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Synchronous Multiplayer:** Console players invest in dedicated sessions and expect real-time interaction. *Justification:* Hardware (low-latency networks, controllers) supports this; social bonds are stronger when formed in real time. *Example:* *Halo Infinite* multiplayer.

2. **Cinematic Narrative Arc:** Console sessions last 1–3 hours, allowing for immersive storytelling. *Justification:* Fixed hardware enables high-fidelity motion capture and orchestral scores. *Example:* *The Last of Us Part II*.

3. **Progressive Mastery Curve:** Players expect skill growth over time. *Justification:* The learning investment justifies the \$60–\$70 price point. *Example:* *Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice*.

4. **Hardware-Exclusive Optimization:** Console devs target fixed hardware specs. *Justification:* Allows engineering optimizations (e.g., PS5 SSD reduces *Spider-Man 2* load times to <2 seconds).

5. **Premium / Subscription Monetization:** Players pay upfront or via subscription. *Justification:* Aligns with the high development cost (**\$100M–\$300M** for AAA titles) and player commitment model.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark per pillar (0.5 definition + 0.5 justification) = 5 marks + 2 marks for synthesis. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A console game has an engagement decay constant $\lambda = 0.005$ per day. Calculate:
- (i) The **half-life** of player engagement.
- (ii) The engagement $E(t)$ after **90 days** if $E_0 = 100$ units.
- (iii) Comment on what this means for the developer's content release strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Half-Life Calculation:**

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\lambda}$$

Substitute $\lambda = 0.005$.

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.005}$$

$$t_{1/2} = 138.6 \text{ days}$$

This means half the initial engagement is lost after ~138 days. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Engagement After 90 Days:**

$$E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substitute $E_0 = 100$, $\lambda = 0.005$, $t = 90$.

$$E(90) = 100 \cdot e^{-0.005 \times 90}$$

$$E(90) = 100 \cdot e^{-0.45}$$

$$E(90) = 100 \cdot 0.6376$$

$$E(90) = 63.76 \text{ units}$$

So engagement drops to **~63.8%** of original after 90 days. **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Strategy Comment:**

Since the half-life is ~138 days, the developer must release **major content drops (DLCs, expansions) every 90–120 days** to "reset" the engagement curve before it decays past the critical 50% threshold. This is why live-service console games (e.g., *Destiny 2*) follow a **~3-month seasonal cadence**. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Always include units (%, days, \$). Students who write "ARPDAU = 0.30" without the **\$** lose 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Formula Misuse:** $D_1$ and $D_7$ retention are **percentages**, not fractions. Forgetting to multiply by 100 is a common error.
> 3. **Confusing Casual and Console Examples:** Examiners deduct up to 1 mark if a student cites a console game (e.g., *Call of Duty*) as an example for casual design and vice versa.
> 4. **Skipping Justifications:** For 7-mark questions, every pillar MUST have a definition **AND** a justification. Definitions alone fetch only 50% marks.
> 5. **Order of Operations in ARPDAU:** Always divide Total Revenue by DAU, never the reverse.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — CASUAL vs. CONSOLE**

- ✅ **Casual** = Short sessions (3–15 min), mobile/web, low learning curve, F2P with ads/IAP.
- ✅ **Console** = Long sessions (30–180 min), dedicated hardware, steep learning curve, premium pricing.
- ✅ **Casual Psychology** = Skinner's Variable Reinforcement; **Console Psychology** = Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory.
- ✅ **Casual Multiplayer** = Asynchronous; **Console Multiplayer** = Synchronous.
- ✅ **Retention Benchmarks:** $D_1$ = 35–45% (casual); $D_7$ = 12–18% (casual).
- ✅ **ARPDAU Range:** Casual = **\$0.05–\$0.30**; Console = N/A (premium model).
- ✅ **Engagement Decay:** $E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$; half-life $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\lambda}$.
- ✅ **Flow Channel Width:** Casual optimizes for wide, short channels per level; Console optimizes for deep, long channels across the campaign.
- ✅ **Casual Pillar Verbs:** *Immediate, Asynchronous, Vertical, Episodic.*
- ✅ **Console Pillar Verbs:** *Synchronous, Cinematic, Mastery, Hardware-Exclusive, Premium.*
- ✅ **Half-life of Casual Engagement** ≈ 10 days (drives weekly updates).
- ✅ **Half-life of Console Engagement** ≈ 138 days (drives seasonal DLC cadence).
- ✅ **CPI Target** for Casual UA = **\$1.5–\$5**.
- ✅ **LTV-CPI Ratio** must exceed **3:1** for casual UA profitability.
- ✅ **K-Factor > 1** = viral self-sustaining growth (casual KPI).
- ✅ **Always cite real examples** in board answers (Angry Birds, Candy Crush, God of War, Zelda).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Apps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT637 - DESIGN PROCESS AND PERSPECTIVE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building Apps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Building Apps** in the context of Design Process and Perspective refers to the **systematic, user-centered methodology of conceptualizing, designing, prototyping, testing, and deploying software applications** (mobile, web, or desktop) by integrating design thinking principles, user experience (UX) frameworks, information architecture, and iterative development cycles.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Design Process and Perspective*, Building Apps is treated as the **practical synthesis phase** where the conceptual frameworks learned in Modules 1–3 (Empathy, Define, Ideate, Prototype) are operationalized into a tangible, testable, and deployable digital product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**:
- The **blueprint** is the wireframe,
- The **3D model** is the prototype,
- The **construction crew** is the development team,
- The **homeowner feedback** is user testing,
- The **final handover** is deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as no good architect skips the blueprint phase, no good app designer skips **research, wireframing, and prototyping** before writing a single line of code.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Wireframe** | Low-fidelity skeletal blueprint of an app screen showing layout and structure. |
| **Prototype** | Interactive simulation of the final product used for usability testing. |
| **Information Architecture (IA)** | Structural design of app content for intuitive findability. |
| **User Flow** | Step-by-step path a user takes to complete a task within the app. |
| **MVP (Minimum Viable Product)** | The simplest version of an app that delivers core value to early adopters. |
| **Design System** | A collection of reusable components, guidelines, and principles for consistent design. |
| **Heuristic Evaluation** | A usability inspection method based on **Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics**. |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework expects students to demonstrate **CO4 (Apply design process to build application prototypes)** — the topic directly maps to this Course Outcome.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The App Building Lifecycle (KTU 5-Phase Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes "Building Apps" into the following operational phases:

1. **Phase 1 — Discover & Research**
   - Conduct user interviews, surveys, and competitive analysis.
   - Build **empathy maps**, **personas**, and **problem statements**.

2. **Phase 2 — Define & Architect**
   - Translate research into actionable **user requirements**.
   - Construct **Information Architecture (IA)** using card sorting and sitemaps.
   - Define the **user journey maps**.

3. **Phase 3 — Design & Prototype**
   - Create **low-fidelity wireframes** (sketches → digital wireframes).
   - Progress to **high-fidelity prototypes** with full visual design.
   - Apply **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** and **WCAG accessibility guidelines**.

4. **Phase 4 — Test & Iterate**
   - Conduct **usability testing** with **5–7 users** (per Nielsen's research).
   - Measure **task success rate**, **time-on-task**, and **System Usability Scale (SUS)** scores.
   - Iterate designs based on feedback.

5. **Phase 5 — Build, Deploy & Measure**
   - Hand off to development with **design specifications**.
   - Deploy to app stores.
   - Measure KPIs: **retention**, **engagement**, **conversion rate**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Phase

- **Why research first?** Skipping research leads to building features nobody wants — a primary cause of app failure (cited in **Standish Group CHAOS Report** as the #1 reason for project failure).
- **Why wireframe before designing?** Wireframes are 10× faster to modify than pixel-perfect designs.
- **Why prototype?** Prototypes cost **100× less** to fix usability issues than post-deployment fixes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **Rule of 5–7 Users** in usability testing (Jakob Nielsen, 2000) states that **5 users** uncover approximately **85% of usability issues**. Increasing to 15 users only marginally improves detection (≈ 95%).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Metric | Formula | Unit / Range | Purpose |
|--------|---------|--------------|---------|
| **Task Success Rate (TSR)** | $TSR = \frac{T_{success}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** (0–100) | Measures task completion efficiency. |
| **Average Time-on-Task (ATT)** | $ATT = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | **seconds** | Measures task efficiency. |
| **Error Rate (ER)** | $ER = \frac{E_{total}}{I_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measures interaction quality. |
| **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $\sum_{score_{1}}^{score_{10}} (\text{adjusted item score})$ | **0–100** | Standard usability score; > **68** = above average. |
| **Conversion Rate (CR)** | $CR = \frac{C_{goal}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measures goal completion. |
| **Retention Rate (RR)** | $RR = \frac{U_{end} - U_{new}}{U_{start}} \times 100$ | **%** | Day-1, Day-7, Day-30 retention metrics. |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \frac{(G - C)}{C} \times 100$ | **%** | Business value of the app. |
| **WCAG Contrast Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$ | **Ratio (1:1 to 21:1)** | AA: ≥ **4.5:1** for normal text. |
| **Heuristic Severity Rating (HSR)** | $HSR = \sum (Frequency \times Impact \times Persistence)$ | **0–4 scale** | Per Nielsen severity scale. |
| **App Performance Index (API)** | $API = w_1 \cdot U + w_2 \cdot P + w_3 \cdot A$ | **Weighted score** | Where U=Usability, P=Performance, A=Accessibility. |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use absolute values expressed with `\vert` notation to maintain markdown table integrity. **SUS ≥ 68** is the KTU-cited benchmark for "good" usability (Bangor, Kortum & Miller, 2009).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare apps** use **TSR** and **ER** metrics to ensure patients complete critical workflows (e.g., medication reminders).
- **E-commerce apps** prioritize **CR** and **RR** for revenue modeling.
- **Banking apps** mandate **WCAG 2.1 AA** compliance for legal accessibility.
- **IoT/Embedded apps** apply **API** to balance usability with real-time performance constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SUS (System Usability Scale) Scoring Algorithm

The SUS uses a **10-item Likert questionnaire** (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree). Here is the exhaustive step-by-step scoring logic the KTU examiner expects:

**Step 1:** For **odd-numbered items** (1, 3, 5, 7, 9), subtract 1 from the user's score.

$$\text{Score}_{odd} = X_{user} - 1$$

**Step 2:** For **even-numbered items** (2, 4, 6, 8, 10), subtract the user's score from 5.

$$\text{Score}_{even} = 5 - X_{user}$$

**Step 3:** Sum all 10 adjusted scores.

$$\text{SUS}_{raw} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{Score}_{adjusted,i}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by **2.5** to normalize to a 0–100 scale.

$$\text{SUS}_{final} = 2.5 \times \text{SUS}_{raw}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Odd items measure *positive* usability (higher = better), even items measure *negative* usability. The subtraction normalizes both directions upward, and the 2.5 multiplier rescales the maximum 40-point sum to 100.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Usability Test Analysis

**Scenario:** A KTU student tests a prototype with **6 users** on the task "Book a cab." The recorded data is:

| User | Task Time (s) | Success? | Errors |
|------|---------------|----------|--------|
| 1    | 45            | Yes      | 0      |
| 2    | 62            | Yes      | 1      |
| 3    | 80            | No       | 3      |
| 4    | 50            | Yes      | 0      |
| 5    | 55            | Yes      | 2      |
| 6    | 48            | Yes      | 0      |

**Step 1: Calculate Task Success Rate (TSR)**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{success} &= 5 \text{ (Users 1, 2, 4, 5, 6)} \\
T_{total} &= 6 \\
TSR &= \frac{5}{6} \times 100 = 83.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Average Time-on-Task (ATT)**

$$\begin{aligned}
ATT &= \frac{45 + 62 + 80 + 50 + 55 + 48}{6} \\
&= \frac{340}{6} \\
&= 56.67 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Error Rate (ER)**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 0 + 1 + 3 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 6 \\
I_{total} &= \text{Number of interactions per task (assume 4)} \times 6 = 24 \\
ER &= \frac{6}{24} \times 100 = 25\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Analytical Matrix — Interpretation**

| Metric | Value | Benchmark | Verdict |
|--------|-------|-----------|---------|
| **TSR** | 83.33% | ≥ **78%** | Acceptable |
| **ATT** | 56.67s | ≤ **60s** | Within range |
| **ER** | 25% | ≤ **10%** | **Fails** — redesign required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The error rate exceeding **10%** is a strong KTU-style signal to **revisit the prototype's interaction design** before proceeding to high-fidelity mockups.

### 3.3 Derivation: Heuristic Severity Rating

For each usability issue found, assign:
- **Frequency** (F): How often the problem occurs? → Scale **0–4**
- **Impact** (I): How severely does it affect the user? → Scale **0–4**
- **Persistence** (P): Is it a one-time or recurring barrier? → Scale **0–4**

$$\text{Heuristic Severity Rating (HSR)} = \frac{F + I + P}{3}$$

**Interpretation Scale:**

| HSR Range | Severity | Action |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| 0.0 – 0.5 | Not a usability problem | No action needed |
| 0.6 – 1.5 | Cosmetic problem | Fix if time permits |
| 1.6 – 2.5 | Minor usability problem | Should be fixed |
| 2.6 – 3.5 | Major usability problem | **Must be fixed** |
| 3.6 – 4.0 | Usability catastrophe | **Release blocker** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: App Building Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Brief] --> B[Phase 1: User Research]
    B --> B1[Empathy Maps]
    B --> B2[User Personas]
    B --> B3[Competitive Analysis]
    B1 --> C[Phase 2: Define]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Problem Statement]
    C --> C2[User Stories]
    C --> C3[Information Architecture]
    C1 --> D[Phase 3: Design and Prototype]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Low-Fidelity Wireframes]
    D --> D2[Style Guide and Design System]
    D --> D3[High-Fidelity Prototypes]
    D1 --> E[Phase 4: Usability Testing]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Task Success Rate Analysis]
    E --> E2[SUS Score Analysis]
    E --> E3[Iterate Fixes]
    E1 --> F[Phase 5: Build and Deploy]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Developer Handoff]
    F --> F2[App Store Deployment]
    F --> F3[Post-Launch Analytics]
    F3 --> B
    F3 --> G[End: Product Live]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: App Architecture Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Client[Client Side]
        UI[UI Layer: Views and Widgets]
        Logic[Business Logic Layer]
        State[State Management Module]
        Cache[Local Cache and Storage]
    end
    subgraph Network[Network Layer]
        API[RESTful API Gateway]
        Auth[Authentication Service]
    end
    subgraph Server[Server Side]
        App[Application Server]
        DB[(Database)]
        Analytics[Analytics Engine]
    end
    UI --> Logic
    Logic --> State
    State --> Cache
    Logic --> API
    API --> Auth
    Auth --> App
    App --> DB
    App --> Analytics
    Analytics -.feedback loop.-> UI
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (Visual Summary)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Nielsen 10 Heuristics))
    H1[Visibility of System Status]
    H2[Match Real World]
    H3[User Control and Freedom]
    H4[Consistency and Standards]
    H5[Error Prevention]
    H6[Recognition vs Recall]
    H7[Flexibility and Efficiency]
    H8[Aesthetic Minimalist Design]
    H9[Help Users Recognize Errors]
    H10[Help and Documentation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **raw uppercase labels** to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards. No markdown formatting is embedded inside quoted node labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**Q1. Define the term "Minimum Viable Product (MVP)" in the context of app building. Why is it considered a strategic design decision?**
*(Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember + Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
A **Minimum Viable Product (MVP)** is the earliest version of an app that contains only the **core features** necessary to deliver the primary value proposition to early users and gather validated learning. It is considered strategic because it (i) **minimizes development cost and time**, (ii) enables **real-user feedback** before full investment, (iii) **de-risks** product-market fit, and (iv) supports the **Lean Startup methodology** of "Build–Measure–Learn." *(Word count: ~65 | Marks: 3)*

---

**Q2. List any **three** of Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics and briefly explain their relevance in app design.**
*(Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember + Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Visibility of System Status** — The app should always inform the user about what is going on (e.g., loading spinners, progress bars).
2. **Error Prevention** — Design should proactively prevent errors (e.g., disabling submit button until a valid form is filled).
3. **Recognition rather than Recall** — The app should minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible (e.g., icons with labels).

*(Any three with brief explanation: 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern | 14 Marks | ~12–15 pages answer]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B*

**(a)** Describe the **complete app building lifecycle** as per the KTU 2024 Design Process framework. Explain each phase with a relevant example of a **food delivery app** (e.g., Zomato-style). *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The KTU-aligned app building lifecycle consists of **5 phases**:

1. **Phase 1 — Discover & Research:** Conduct user interviews with 10–15 target users (e.g., college students, working professionals). Build **empathy maps** to capture their pain points (long wait times, unclear menus). Perform **competitive analysis** of Swiggy and Zomato.

2. **Phase 2 — Define & Architect:** Create **user personas** (e.g., "Riya, 22, hostel student"). Formulate **problem statements** like *"Riya needs a quick way to find affordable veg meals under ₹100 near her campus."* Design the **Information Architecture**: Home → Cuisine → Restaurant → Menu → Cart → Checkout.

3. **Phase 3 — Design & Prototype:** Begin with **paper wireframes** of the home screen and checkout flow. Build **high-fidelity prototypes** in Figma with the brand color palette (red, white) and apply **Nielsen's heuristics** (e.g., H1: show real-time order tracking).

4. **Phase 4 — Test & Iterate:** Run **moderated usability tests** with 5 users. Measure **TSR, ATT, and SUS**. If SUS < 68, iterate the prototype (e.g., simplify filter UI).

5. **Phase 5 — Build, Deploy & Measure:** Hand off the design system to developers. Deploy to Google Play and App Store. Track **Day-1 retention, conversion rate, and order frequency**.

---

**(b)** A startup is building a **fitness tracking app**. During usability testing with **5 users**, the following SUS scores were recorded: **72, 65, 80, 58, 75**. Calculate the **final SUS score** and interpret its usability grade. Also, suggest **two design improvements** based on the result. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Adjust each score per SUS rules.

| User | Item 1 (odd) | Item 2 (even) | Item 3 (odd) | Item 4 (even) | Item 5 (odd) | Item 6 (even) | Item 7 (odd) | Item 8 (even) | Item 9 (odd) | Item 10 (even) |
|------|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1    | 4            | 2             | 3            | 1             | 4            | 2             | 3            | 1             | 4            | 2              |
| 2    | 3            | 1             | 2            | 3             | 4            | 1             | 2            | 2             | 3            | 2              |
| 3    | 4            | 1             | 4            | 1             | 4            | 1             | 3            | 1             | 4            | 1              |
| 4    | 2            | 3             | 1            | 4             | 2            | 3             | 1            | 4             | 2            | 3              |
| 5    | 3            | 1             | 3            | 1             | 4            | 2             | 3            | 1             | 3            | 2              |

**Step 2:** Sum the adjusted scores per user (illustrative; using standard SUS conversion).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SUS}_{user} &= (X_1 - 1) + (5 - X_2) + (X_3 - 1) + (5 - X_4) + (X_5 - 1) + (5 - X_6) \\
&\quad + (X_7 - 1) + (5 - X_8) + (X_9 - 1) + (5 - X_{10})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Final SUS = **2.5 × Σ adjusted scores**.

> **Note to examiner:** The full computation yields an **average SUS ≈ 70**, which is **above the 68 benchmark** → "Good" usability (Grade C in the Sauro-Lewis curved grading scale).

**Step 4: Design Improvements:**
1. **Simplify onboarding** (User 4 scored low — possibly confused by initial setup). Add **progress indicators** (H1: Visibility of System Status).
2. **Improve icon recognizability** with text labels (H6: Recognition vs. Recall).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Wireframing, Prototyping, and Design Systems** in app building. Compare **low-fidelity** vs **high-fidelity** prototypes with **one real-world example each**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

- **Wireframing:** A **2D skeletal layout** of an app screen focusing on structure, hierarchy, and content placement — no colors, typography, or imagery. Created using tools like Balsamiq or paper sketches.
- **Prototyping:** An **interactive, clickable simulation** that mimics the final app's behavior. Used for usability testing before coding.
- **Design System:** A **centralized library** of UI components (buttons, cards, typography, color tokens) and **usage guidelines** that ensures design consistency across screens and teams. Examples: Google's Material Design, Apple's Human Interface Guidelines.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Low-Fidelity Prototype | High-Fidelity Prototype |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Visual Detail** | Grayscale, boxes, lorem ipsum | Pixel-perfect, real content, branding |
| **Interactivity** | Limited (tap-through flows) | Full interactions, transitions, animations |
| **Time to Create** | Hours to 1 day | Days to weeks |
| **Use Case** | Early ideation, internal review | Stakeholder demo, final usability testing |
| **Example** | Paper sketch of a login screen | Figma mockup of a fully designed Spotify playlist screen |

---

**(b)** Define **Information Architecture (IA)**. With a suitable **sitemap diagram** (textual representation), design the IA for a **college ERP mobile app** with the following modules: **Attendance, Marks, Timetable, Fee Payment, Notifications, Profile**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** **Information Architecture (IA)** is the **structural design of an app's content** that organizes, labels, and arranges information in a way that supports **findability and usability** (Rosenfeld & Morville, 1998).

**Textual Sitemap for College ERP App:**

```
College ERP App
│
├── Home Dashboard
│     ├── Attendance Summary
│     ├── Today's Timetable
│     └── Latest Notifications
│
├── Academics
│     ├── Attendance
│     │     ├── Subject-wise View
│     │     └── Monthly Report
│     ├── Marks
│     │     ├── Internal Marks
│     │     └── Semester Results
│     └── Timetable
│           ├── Weekly View
│           └── Calendar View
│
├── Finance
│     └── Fee Payment
│           ├── Pay Now
│           ├── Payment History
│           └── Receipts
│
├── Notifications
│     ├── Announcements
│     └── Alerts
│
└── Profile
      ├── Personal Details
      ├── Edit Profile
      └── Logout
```

**Design Rationale:**
- **Hierarchical IA** chosen for clarity and ease of navigation.
- **Bottom navigation bar** (Material Design) would host the 4 primary destinations: Home, Academics, Finance, Profile.
- **Card-sorting sessions** with 8–10 students should validate the IA before development.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to multiply SUS by 2.5** → loses 1 mark.
> - Confusing **TSR formula** with **ER formula** is a common error — TS uses `success / total`, ER uses `errors / interactions`.
> - In sitemap questions, **missing the hierarchy levels** (e.g., placing "Monthly Report" as a top-level node) costs 2 marks.
> - Do **not** write generic phrases like *"design is good"* — always cite a **specific Nielsen heuristic** (e.g., H1, H6) to justify design choices.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Building Apps**

- ✅ The **5-phase App Building Lifecycle**: Research → Define → Design → Test → Deploy.
- ✅ **MVP** is the leanest functional version; supports Build–Measure–Learn.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — memorize at least the **first 5** by name and example.
- ✅ **SUS = 2.5 × Σ adjusted scores**; benchmark **> 68 = Good** usability.
- ✅ **TSR = (Success / Total) × 100**; benchmark **≥ 78%**.
- ✅ **ER = (Errors / Total Interactions) × 100**; benchmark **≤ 10%**.
- ✅ **5 users uncover ~85% of usability issues** (Nielsen's Rule).
- ✅ **Wireframes** = low-fidelity structure; **Prototypes** = interactive simulations; **Design Systems** = reusable component libraries.
- ✅ **IA** uses **card sorting, sitemaps, and hierarchy testing** to organize content.
- ✅ **WCAG 2.1 AA** requires **contrast ratio ≥ 4.5:1** for normal text.
- ✅ Always justify design decisions with a **heuristic + user data**, not opinions.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme maps this topic to **CO4**: *"Apply design process to build application prototypes."*
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, use **tables, sub-headings, and diagrams** — KTU examiners reward **structured presentation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Interactive Media for Performance and Public Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT637 - DESIGN PROCESS AND PERSPECTIVE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building Interactive Media for Performance and Public Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interactive Media for Performance and Public Spaces** is the disciplined practice of designing, engineering, and deploying responsive digital systems — encompassing projection mapping, immersive audio-visual installations, sensor-driven environments, kinetic displays, and networked participatory platforms — that engage audiences in live, real-time, site-specific contexts such as theatres, concert halls, galleries, plazas, transit hubs, and urban facades.

In the context of the **Design Process and Perspective (PECNT637)** framework, this topic sits at the intersection of three converging disciplines:

1. **Media Arts & Performance Design** — choreography of light, sound, image, and motion.
2. **Interaction Design (IxD)** — the dialogue between human intent and computational response.
3. **Spatial & Architectural Computing** — the integration of media into the built environment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Living Building"**
> Imagine a building that *watches* you walk by, *listens* to your footsteps, *remembers* your presence, and *responds* by changing its skin — light pulsing where you stand, a melody harmonising with your pace, a projection blooming across its wall. This is not science fiction; it is the core promise of interactive media in public space. The space is no longer a passive container. It becomes a **participant** in the performance.

Where traditional signage **broadcasts** (one-way), interactive media **converses** (bi-directional). The public is no longer a spectator but a **co-author** of the experience.

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standard Parameters

In professional KTU-aligned practice, every interactive installation is benchmarked against the following standard metrics:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Latency** | $\leq \mathbf{50 \text{ ms}}$ | The threshold below which human perception cannot detect delay (Knee of the JND curve). |
| **Frame Rate** | $\mathbf{\geq 30 \text{ fps}}$ (min), $\mathbf{60 \text{ fps}}$ (premium) | Smoothness of visual feedback loop. |
| **Audience Engagement Window** | $\mathbf{15\text{–}90 \text{ seconds}}$ | Average dwell time in public installations (Williamson, 2021). |
| **Sensor Sampling Rate** | $\mathbf{\geq 44.1 \text{ kHz}}$ (audio), $\mathbf{\geq 30 \text{ Hz}}$ (vision) | Nyquist-compliant capture rates. |
| **Ambient Light Tolerance** | $\mathbf{200\text{–}50{,}000 \text{ lux}}$ | Operational luminance range for outdoor displays. |
| **Viewing Cone** | $\mathbf{120^\circ\text{–}160^\circ}$ | Angular field for shared public viewing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **Latency Budget** is the single most critical design constraint in interactive public media. If your total input-to-pixel latency exceeds **100 ms**, the audience perceives the system as "laggy" and disengages, regardless of visual fidelity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Pillar Architecture of Interactive Public Media

Every successful interactive media installation — from a small gallery piece to a city-scale facade — is built on **five interdependent pillars**. Understanding these pillars is essential for the KTU Module 4 examination.

### Pillar 1 — **Perception Layer (Input)**
The system must *sense* the world. Common modalities include:
- **Computer Vision:** RGB cameras, depth sensors (LiDAR, ToF, structured light), thermal imaging.
- **Audio Capture:** Condenser microphones, beam-forming arrays, contact microphones.
- **Tactile & Proximity:** Capacitive sensors, infrared break-beams, ultrasonic rangefinders, RFID/NFC.
- **Biometric & Environmental:** EEG (experimental), CO₂, temperature, ambient light (lux), barometric pressure.

### Pillar 2 — **Computation Layer (Processing)**
Raw sensor data is transformed into meaningful *triggers*. This involves:
- **Signal conditioning** (filtering, normalisation, denoising).
- **Pattern recognition** (skeletal tracking, gesture classification, sentiment analysis).
- **Generative logic** (algorithmic composition, procedural geometry, AI-driven synthesis).

### Pillar 3 — **Mapping Layer (Translation)**
The critical and often under-designed bridge between input and output. A *mapping function* $M$ translates a sensor vector $\mathbf{s}$ into an output parameter vector $\mathbf{o}$:

$$\mathbf{o} = M(\mathbf{s}, t, \mathbf{c})$$

where $t$ is time and $\mathbf{c}$ is the contextual state. Mappings may be:
- **Direct** (1:1, e.g., x-position of body → x-position of light beam).
- **Amplified** (micro-input → macro-output, e.g., breath → 10m light wave).
- **Accumulated** (sum of inputs over time → slow-rising visual state).
- **Probabilistic / Generative** (input seeds a stochastic output, e.g., Markov chains, GANs).

### Pillar 4 — **Expression Layer (Output)**
The system must *manifest* a response. Output modalities:
- **Visual:** Projection mapping, LED matrices, holographic screens, e-ink, laser.
- **Auditory:** Spatial audio (Ambisonics, Wave Field Synthesis), parametric speakers.
- **Haptic & Kinetic:** Vibration motors, pneumatics, robotic arms, moving architecture.
- **Olfactory & Gustatory:** Experimental scent diffusers (Sissakian scent systems).

### Pillar 5 — **Contextual Layer (Frame)**
The site, the audience, the cultural moment. This pillar answers: *Why here? Why now? For whom?*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the core analytical toolkit for Module 4. Memorise the variables, units, and operational ranges.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Key | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **End-to-End Latency** $L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{net} + L_{render}$ | $L$ in **ms** | Budgets system responsiveness. |
| 2 | **Nyquist Sampling Criterion** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sample rate | Prevents aliasing in sensor capture. |
| 3 | **Frame Rate vs. Latency** $\text{Frame Time} = \frac{1}{\text{fps}}$ | seconds per frame | Determines visual smoothness. |
| 4 | **Projection Luminance (Lux on surface)** $E_v = \frac{\Phi_v \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | $\Phi_v$ = luminous flux, $d$ = throw distance | Sizes projectors for ambient conditions. |
| 5 | **Throw Ratio** $TR = \frac{D}{W}$ | $D$ = distance, $W$ = image width | Lens selection for projection mapping. |
| 6 | **Refresh Continuity** $f_{refresh} \geq f_{capture}$ | Hz | Avoids stutter between camera and display. |
| 7 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Sensor and audio fidelity benchmark. |
| 8 | **Mapping Gain** $G_{map} = \frac{\Delta o_{max}}{\Delta s_{max}}$ | dimensionless | Controls sensitivity of input→output response. |
| 9 | **Audience Density Index** $\rho = \frac{N_{people}}{A_{zone}}$ | persons / m² | Sizing interaction zones in public space. |
| 10 | **Viewing Distance (minimum)** $D_{min} \approx 1.5 \cdot H_{screen}$ | metres | Ensures legible pixel pitch from audience. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Markdown Tables:** All vertical bars in mathematical expressions (such as absolute value $\vert x \vert$) have been deliberately replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. This is a mandatory formatting convention for KTU 2024 digital submissions.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility & Application Domains

The five-pillar architecture finds direct application in:

- **Architectural Projection Mapping** — *e.g., facade storytelling on heritage buildings during festivals (think Vivid Sydney, Fête des Lumières).*
- **Participatory Stage Design** — *real-time generative backdrops responsive to dancer movement (think TeamLab, Random International).*
- **Public Information Installations** — *interactive kiosks in transit hubs, smart-city wayfinding totems.*
- **Generative Murals & Floor Projections** — *floor-reactive visuals in museums, retail plazas, children’s hospitals.*
- **Live Concert Visuals** — *audio-reactive VJ systems, real-time lyric visualisation.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Mapping Layer (Pillar 3)** is where the *designer* — not the engineer — exercises the most creative authority. Two installations using identical sensors and projectors can produce wildly different audience experiences based purely on the mapping function $M(\cdot)$. This is the designer's primary lever.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Interactive Media Latency Budget

The **latency budget** is the most exam-relevant derivation in Module 4. The following is the exhaustive, step-by-step derivation that a KTU examiner expects to see in a 14-mark answer.

**Step 1 — Define the system as a four-stage pipeline.**

A real-time interactive media system consists of four sequential stages, each contributing a delay:

$$\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Compute} \rightarrow \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Render} \rightarrow \text{Eye}$$

**Step 2 — Express each stage as a latency term.**

Let:
- $L_{sensor}$ = time from physical event to digitised signal (camera exposure + readout).
- $L_{proc}$ = time from digitised input to computed output (skeleton tracking, audio analysis, etc.).
- $L_{net}$ = time for inter-process / inter-machine communication (OSC, NDI, Spout, etc.).
- $L_{render}$ = time from computed output to emitted photons (frame buffer swap + projector scan-out).

**Step 3 — Sum the stages to obtain total latency.**

$$L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{net} + L_{render}$$

**Step 4 — Add human perceptual delay.**

The complete perceptual loop includes the time for the visual signal to reach the brain and trigger motor response. In closed-loop interaction design, this is modelled as:

$$L_{perceived} = L_{total} + L_{cognition} + L_{motor}$$

For the KTU examination, the standard cognitive-motor delay is given as:

$$L_{cognition} + L_{motor} \approx \mathbf{150\text{–}250 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the perceptual threshold.**

For an interaction to feel "real-time" and embodied, the system latency must remain below the human "Just Noticeable Difference" (JND) curve:

$$L_{total} \leq \mathbf{50 \text{ ms}}$$

If $L_{total} > 100$ ms, the audience begins to perceive the system as asynchronous.

**Step 6 — Numerical Example.**

Suppose a system has:
- $L_{sensor} = 8 \text{ ms}$ (global-shutter machine vision camera at 120 fps).
- $L_{proc} = 12 \text{ ms}$ (skeletal tracking on a dedicated GPU node).
- $L_{net} = 4 \text{ ms}$ (local OSC over gigabit ethernet).
- $L_{render} = 11 \text{ ms}$ (60 fps output, including one frame buffer).

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{net} + L_{render} \\
L_{total} &= 8 + 12 + 4 + 11 \\
L_{total} &= \mathbf{35 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $35 \text{ ms} < 50 \text{ ms}$, this configuration is **embodied-grade** — suitable for dance, instrument-like control, or tight fight-choreography visuals.

## 3.2 Derivation of Projection Mapping Throw Distance

A frequent 14-mark question concerns selecting the correct projector and lens for a given facade. The derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Define throw ratio $TR$.**

$$TR = \frac{D}{W}$$

where:
- $D$ = throw distance (projector lens to surface) in metres.
- $W$ = image width on the surface in metres.

**Step 2 — Solve for throw distance.**

$$D = TR \cdot W$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse square law for illuminance.**

The illuminance $E_v$ on the surface in lux is given by:

$$E_v = \frac{\Phi_v \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

where:
- $\Phi_v$ = luminous flux of the projector in **lumens**.
- $\theta$ = angle between the projector's optical axis and the surface normal.
- $d$ = perpendicular throw distance.

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**

A facade is $W = 12$ m wide. The chosen projector has $TR = 1.2$ and $\Phi_v = 20{,}000$ lumens. The projector is placed on-axis ($\theta = 0^\circ$). Find $D$ and $E_v$.

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= TR \cdot W = 1.2 \times 12 = \mathbf{14.4 \text{ m}} \\
E_v &= \frac{20{,}000 \cdot \cos(0^\circ)}{(14.4)^2} = \frac{20{,}000}{207.36} \approx \mathbf{96.5 \text{ lux}}
\end{aligned}$$

For outdoor dusk viewing on a stone facade, the recommended minimum is **~50 lux on surface**. The design is **marginally acceptable**; a brighter projector or shorter throw is advised for full daylight.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Strategies

The mapping function $M(\mathbf{s}, t, \mathbf{c})$ is the designer's core instrument. The following analytical matrix — frequently examined — classifies mappings by behaviour:

| Mapping Type | Equation Form | Audience Experience | Design Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct (1:1)** | $o_i = k \cdot s_i$ | Immediate, embodied | Boring if too literal |
| **Inverted** | $o_i = -k \cdot s_i$ | Counterintuitive, playful | Confusing without onboarding |
| **Threshold** | $o_i = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } s_i < T \\ 1 & \text{if } s_i \geq T \end{cases}$ | Discrete, "magical" | Dead zones between thresholds |
| **Integrative** | $o_i(t) = \int_0^t s_i(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Builds over time | Drift, runaway accumulation |
| **Probabilistic** | $o_i \sim P(\mathbf{s}, t)$ | Surprise, generative novelty | Unpredictable for narrative work |
| **Networked (Multi-user)** | $o_i = f(s_i^{(1)}, s_i^{(2)}, \ldots, s_i^{(n)})$ | Social, choreographic | Bandwidth, fairness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** In a 14-mark question, you will be expected to draw this table *and* justify why a particular mapping was chosen for a given performance context. The justification (one sentence per row) is what differentiates a 10-mark answer from a full 14-mark answer.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Designing a Floor-Reactive Public Plaza

**Problem Statement:** Design a $10 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m}$ interactive floor in a public plaza that responds to footsteps with light ripples. Assume:
- Audience density $\rho = 0.3$ persons/m².
- Desired latency $L_{total} \leq 50$ ms.
- Operating in dusk ambient light $E_{ambient} = 30$ lux.

**Step 1 — Sensor selection.**
A floor-mounted **time-of-flight (ToF) depth array** with $f_s = 60 \text{ Hz}$ is chosen. This gives $L_{sensor} \approx 16 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 2 — Compute selection.**
An on-site industrial PC with GPU acceleration runs skeletal tracking. $L_{proc} \approx 20 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 3 — Network.**
No network (local). $L_{net} = 0 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 4 — Render.**
Floor LED matrix at 60 fps. $L_{render} \approx 16.7 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 5 — Total latency check.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 16 + 20 + 0 + 16.7 \\
L_{total} &= 52.7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

The total is **just over** the 50 ms target. Mitigation: switch to a 90 Hz ToF sensor ($L_{sensor} \approx 11$ ms), yielding:

$$L_{total} = 11 + 20 + 0 + 16.7 = \mathbf{47.7 \text{ ms}}$$

**Design passes.** The system is now embodied-grade.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — The Five-Pillar Interactive Media Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Public Space Site] --> A2[Audience Member]
    A2 -->|gesture, presence, voice, motion| B1[Sensor Array]
    B1 -->|raw signal| B2[Signal Conditioning]
    B2 -->|cleaned data| C1[Compute Engine]
    C1 -->|pattern detection| C2[Generative Logic]
    C2 -->|output parameters| D1[Mapping Function]
    D1 -->|translated cues| D2[Output Modality]
    D2 -->|light, sound, motion| E1[Audience Feedback]
    E1 -->|perception| A2
    F1[Contextual Brief] -.->|informs| C1
    F1 -.->|informs| D1
    F1 -.->|informs| D2
    G1[Latency Budget] -.->|monitors| B1
    G1 -.->|monitors| C1
    G1 -.->|monitors| D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows (A1 → A2 → B1 → ...) form the **live data path** — the closed loop of perception and response. The dotted arrows (F1, G1) represent the **design-time constraints** — the brief and the latency budget that govern the system's tuning. The loop closes at E1 → A2, where the audience's perception of the output becomes the next input to the system.

## 4.2 Stage-Gate Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Site Survey] --> P2[Audience Ethnography]
    P2 --> P3[Conceptual Brief]
    P3 --> P4[Sensor Selection]
    P4 --> P5[Mapping Design]
    P5 --> P6[Prototype Loop]
    P6 --> P7{Acceptance Test}
    P7 -->|fail| P5
    P7 -->|pass| P8[Deployment]
    P8 --> P9[Live Monitoring]
    P9 --> P10[Post Occupancy Evaluation]
    P10 -->|lessons learned| P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **spiral design process** for interactive public media. The critical insight is the **feedback loop** from P10 (Post-Occupancy Evaluation) back to P3 (Conceptual Brief). Unlike a one-shot construction project, interactive installations **evolve** with their audience — data from prior deployments informs the next iteration. This cyclical structure is a high-yield topic for KTU Part B questions.

## 4.3 Sensor-to-Output Modality Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR_LAYER
    S1[RGB Camera]
    S2[Depth Sensor]
    S3[Microphone Array]
    S4[Capacitive Touch]
    S5[IMU Wearable]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER
    PROC1[Skeleton Tracking]
    PROC2[FFT Audio Analysis]
    PROC3[Touch Point Map]
    PROC4[Orientation Filter]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
    OUT1[Projection Mapping]
    OUT2[LED Floor]
    OUT3[Spatial Audio]
    OUT4[Haptic Vest]
    OUT5[Kinetic Sculpture]
    end
    S1 --> PROC1 --> OUT1
    S2 --> PROC1 --> OUT2
    S3 --> PROC2 --> OUT3
    S4 --> PROC3 --> OUT1
    S5 --> PROC4 --> OUT4
    S2 --> PROC1 --> OUT5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to *propose a system* for a given performance context, this three-layer decomposition (Sensor → Process → Output) is the canonical structure. Sketching this diagram in your answer — even roughly — immediately signals to the examiner that you understand the **architectural separation of concerns** in interactive media design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Define the term "Latency Budget" as used in interactive media system design. Why is it considered the single most critical design constraint?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Latency Budget** is the total permissible delay allocated across the four sequential stages of a real-time interactive media pipeline — sensing, processing, network transmission, and rendering — expressed mathematically as:
>
> $$L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{net} + L_{render}$$
>
> It is considered the most critical constraint because the human perceptual system can detect delays exceeding **~50 ms**. Beyond this threshold, the audience perceives the system as asynchronous or "laggy," which breaks the **embodied feedback loop** essential to interactive performance. Unlike visual fidelity or colour accuracy — which degrade gradually — latency failure is binary: the system is either responsive or it is broken. Therefore, every design decision in interactive media must be validated against the latency budget before any aesthetic or content consideration.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between "Direct Mapping" and "Probabilistic Mapping" in the context of interactive media. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Direct Mapping | Probabilistic Mapping |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Equation** | $o_i = k \cdot s_i$ | $o_i \sim P(\mathbf{s}, t)$ |
> | **Determinism** | Fully deterministic | Stochastic / generative |
> | **Audience Feel** | Embodied, intuitive, "instrument-like" | Surprising, generative, "conversation-like" |
>
> **Example of Direct Mapping:** A dancer's hand position is mapped 1:1 to the horizontal position of a light beam. The dancer feels they are *holding* the light.
>
> **Example of Probabilistic Mapping:** A passer-by's silhouette seeds a generative audio-visual composition using a Markov chain, so the same pose may produce different outputs on different occasions. The audience feels they are *co-composing* with the system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> In 14-mark questions on this topic, students **commonly lose 3–5 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing "sensor" with "input modality"** — A camera is a sensor; *sight* is the modality. Naming the technology is not the same as naming the human channel.
> 2. **Omitting numerical justification** — Saying "the system is low-latency" without computing $L_{total}$ is treated as an unsubstantiated claim. Always show the calculation.
> 3. **Ignoring the audience** — A "beautiful" technical answer that does not address *who* the work is for, *why* the site matters, and *how* the public will discover the interaction loses marks under the Contextual Layer (Pillar 5).
> 4. **Forgetting safety, accessibility, and ethics** — Public installations must consider crowd safety, ADA / accessible-design compliance, and consent (especially with biometric sensing). Examiners explicitly look for this.

---

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Five-Pillar Architecture** of interactive media for performance and public spaces. For each pillar, identify the designer's primary concern and give one concrete example.

**(b) [7 marks]** A municipal cultural authority has commissioned an interactive media installation for a $30 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ heritage facade. The work must:
- Respond to pedestrian movement in the plaza below.
- Operate between dusk (ambient $E_{ambient} = 20$ lux) and full dark.
- Maintain a latency of $\leq 50 \text{ ms}$.
- Use a 20,000-lumen projector with throw ratio $TR = 1.5$.

Compute the required throw distance, the resulting surface illuminance, and propose a sensor-and-compute pipeline that meets the latency budget.

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Seven Marks**

The Five-Pillar Architecture decomposes any interactive media installation into:

1. **Perception Layer (Input) — Designer's concern:** *What human signals matter?*
   - Example: A ceiling-mounted depth camera captures pedestrian silhouettes from above.

2. **Computation Layer (Processing) — Designer's concern:** *What does the signal mean?*
   - Example: A GPU node runs skeleton-tracking software (e.g., MediaPipe, Nuitrack) to extract 17 skeletal keypoints per person.

3. **Mapping Layer (Translation) — Designer's concern:** *How is meaning transformed into expression?*
   - Example: The horizontal centroid of all detected people modulates the hue of a slow gradient sweep on the facade.

4. **Expression Layer (Output) — Designer's concern:** *How does the work manifest in the world?*
   - Example: A high-lumen projector warps a generative texture across the stone facade using a calibrated mesh.

5. **Contextual Layer (Frame) — Designer's concern:** *Why here, why now, for whom?*
   - Example: The installation references local heritage motifs (e.g., temple geometry, river patterns) to root the work in the site's cultural memory.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Seven Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute throw distance.**

$$D = TR \cdot W = 1.5 \times 30 = \mathbf{45 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute surface illuminance on-axis.**

$$E_v = \frac{20{,}000 \cdot \cos(0^\circ)}{(45)^2} = \frac{20{,}000}{2025} \approx \mathbf{9.88 \text{ lux}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ambient.**

The required surface contrast at dusk is approximately $3:1$ over ambient. Required $E_v \geq 3 \times 20 = 60$ lux. **The 9.88 lux is insufficient.** Recommendation: shorten throw to ~15 m using a wide-angle lens, or upgrade to a $\geq 120{,}000$ lumen projector.

**Step 4 — Propose a latency-compliant pipeline.**

| Stage | Technology | Latency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensor | Global-shutter stereo camera @ 120 fps | $8 \text{ ms}$ |
| Processing | On-site GPU with optimised tracker | $15 \text{ ms}$ |
| Network | Local OSC over Cat6 | $3 \text{ ms}$ |
| Render | MadMapper with edge-blending, 60 fps output | $11 \text{ ms}$ |

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 8 + 15 + 3 + 11 \\
L_{total} &= \mathbf{37 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}$$

This satisfies the **50 ms** requirement with a **13 ms safety margin**.

---

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **projection mapping** and **LED matrix installations** as output modalities for interactive public media. Discuss the trade-offs in terms of luminance, scalability, maintenance, and audience perception.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design a **mapping function** for a live dance performance in which a solo dancer's gestures drive a generative backdrop. Specify:
1. At least three distinct input parameters $\mathbf{s} = (s_1, s_2, s_3)$ drawn from the dancer's body.
2. At least three output parameters $\mathbf{o} = (o_1, o_2, o_3)$ driving the visual composition.
3. The form of the mapping $M(\mathbf{s}, t)$.
4. Justify your mapping choice in terms of choreographic intention.

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Seven Marks**

| Dimension | Projection Mapping | LED Matrix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Luminance** | Limited; struggles in daylight (typically $\leq 100$ lux on surface outdoors). | Excellent; can exceed $5{,}000$ nits, fully daylight-capable. |
| **Scalability** | Easily scales to giant facades; one projector covers huge area. | Modular; scales by tiling, but pixel pitch grows with distance. |
| **Maintenance** | Lamps/lasers degrade; alignment drift common; calibration critical. | Solid-state; long lifetime ($>50{,}000$ hrs); individual pixel failures. |
| **Audience Perception** | Ethereal, "painted on" the architecture; reads as image. | Emissive, "of the architecture"; reads as surface, light, or skin. |
| **Cost at Scale** | Lower for very large areas. | Higher, but more robust. |
| **Site Sensitivity** | Can damage heritage surfaces with heat; mounting is critical. | Requires structural mounting; often flush with new builds. |

**Conclusion:** Projection mapping is preferred for **heritage, irregular, or temporary** installations where the architecture itself is the canvas. LED matrices are preferred for **permanent, high-traffic, daylight** installations where robustness and brightness dominate.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Seven Marks**

**1. Input parameters** (from dancer's body via on-stage IMU sensors and a depth camera):
- $s_1$ = vertical extension of the right arm (range 0 to 1, normalised).
- $s_2$ = horizontal velocity of the torso centroid (m/s).
- $s_3$ = hand-closure ratio — the degree to which both hands are clasped (0 to 1).

**2. Output parameters** (driving a TouchDesigner / Notch generative composition projected behind the dancer):
- $o_1$ = vertical position of a horizon line in the projection.
- $o_2$ = density of a particle field overlay.
- $o_3$ = hue rotation of the dominant colour palette.

**3. Mapping function** (a non-linear, integrative, and probabilistic composite):

$$o_1 = k_1 \cdot s_1 + A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$$

$$o_2 = \alpha \cdot s_2^2 + \beta \cdot \int_0^t s_2(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$o_3 = \text{atan2}\!\left(s_3, \, 1 - s_3\right) \cdot \frac{180^\circ}{\pi} + \text{noise}(t)$$

**4. Choreographic justification:**

- $s_1 \rightarrow o_1$ uses a **direct mapping with a slow oscillation overlay**, so the horizon literally rises with the dancer's reach, but breathes autonomously — the dancer feels they are *lifting* a sky, but the sky has its own quiet life.
- $s_2 \rightarrow o_2$ uses a **quadratic and integrative** mapping, so stillness produces few particles but rapid movement erupts a dense storm that *persists* — the audience sees the dancer's accumulated energy, not just the present moment. This rewards sustained intensity.
- $s_3 \rightarrow o_3$ uses an **atan2-based hue rotation** so that the act of clasping hands (closing $s_3 \rightarrow 1$) drives hue through its full cycle, symbolically tying *intention* to *transformation*. The added `noise(t)` term ensures the same gesture never produces the same colour twice, preserving liveness.

This mapping honours the choreographer's likely intent: the dancer is **not a controller** but a **co-author** with a system that remembers, anticipates, and surprises.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Building Interactive Media for Performance and Public Spaces**

- [ ] **Five-Pillar Architecture** — Perception, Computation, Mapping, Expression, Context. Always answer with these five layers.
- [ ] **Latency Budget Equation** — $L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{net} + L_{render}$. The **50 ms** threshold is embodied-grade; **100 ms** is the absolute ceiling.
- [ ] **Nyquist Sampling** — $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$. Applies to audio (44.1 kHz) and vision (30 Hz minimum).
- [ ] **Throw Ratio** — $TR = D / W$. Solve for distance, then verify illuminance via the inverse-square relationship.
- [ ] **Mapping Taxonomy** — Direct, Inverted, Threshold, Integrative, Probabilistic, Networked. Justify your choice in every answer.
- [ ] **Pillar 3 (Mapping) is the designer's primary lever**, not the technology. Sensors and projectors are interchangeable; the mapping is the art.
- [ ] **Always quantify.** Saying "low latency" without numbers is incomplete. Saying "low brightness" without lumens is incomplete.
- [ ] **Audience dwell time** in public installations is typically **15–90 seconds**; design for the *first impression*, not the deep dive.
- [ ] **Safety, accessibility, and consent** are non-negotiable in public-space work. Biometric sensing requires explicit signage and opt-in design.
- [ ] **Post-Occupancy Evaluation (POE)** closes the design loop. Real installations evolve; treat deployment as the *start*, not the *end*, of the process.
- [ ] **Spiral design** is the correct mental model — not waterfall. Each iteration refines the mapping based on live audience behaviour.
- [ ] **Contextual Layer (Pillar 5)** is what differentiates a *university project* from a *professional installation*. Always answer *why here, why now, for whom*.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PECNT637 Design Process and Perspective**
