# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Website (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *website* is a collection of publicly accessible, interlinked web pages that share a common domain name and are served from one or more web servers. The process of **building a website** encompasses the systematic application of the design process — *empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test, and deploy* — to plan, structure, design, develop, and maintain a digital product that delivers content, services, or experiences to end users over the World Wide Web using standard protocols (primarily **HTTP/HTTPS**).

In the context of the **Design Process and Perspective** course, *building websites* is treated as an integrated design problem that merges **information architecture**, **interaction design (IxD)**, **user experience (UX)**, **user interface (UI)**, and **front-end/back-end engineering** into a single user-centered deliverable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Metaphor"

Imagine you are opening a **restaurant** in a new city. Building a website is exactly like this:

| Restaurant Element | Website Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Building & Location** | Domain name + Web hosting | How users *find* and *reach* your site |
| **Interior Layout** | Information architecture & navigation | How users *move* through content |
| **Menu & Signage** | UI components, typography, color | How users *understand* what is offered |
| **Service Style** | Interaction design, micro-interactions | How users *feel* during the visit |
| **Kitchen** | Back-end (server, database, APIs) | How content is *prepared* and *served* |
| **Dining Area** | Front-end (HTML, CSS, JS) | What users *see* and *interact with* |
| **Customer Reviews** | Analytics, user testing, iteration | Continuous *improvement* |

Just as a bad restaurant has great food but poor signage (usability failure) or beautiful interiors but slow service (interaction failure), a website can fail at any layer of the stack — even if its code is perfect.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The design process for a website is **non-linear and iterative**. Unlike traditional engineering where the waterfall model dominates, web design follows a **double-diamond / agile hybrid** — *discover → define → design → develop → deploy → test → refine*.

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Syllabus-Aligned)

The following terms are **assessable** in KTU 2024 ESE and must be mastered verbatim:

- **Domain Name:** The human-readable address of a website (e.g., `www.ktu.ac.in`), resolved via the **Domain Name System (DNS)** into an IP address.
- **Web Hosting:** The service of storing website files on a server connected to the Internet so they can be requested via HTTP/HTTPS.
- **Front-End (Client-Side):** The part of a website the user directly interacts with — built using **HTML, CSS, and JavaScript** and executed by the browser.
- **Back-End (Server-Side):** The logic, database, and APIs that run on a remote server and respond to client requests.
- **Responsive Design:** An approach where the layout adapts fluidly to the screen size and orientation of the viewing device.
- **Wireframe:** A low-fidelity structural blueprint of a webpage showing layout and content hierarchy without visual styling.
- **Prototype:** An interactive, mid-to-high fidelity simulation of the final website used for usability testing.
- **Accessibility (a11y):** The practice of designing websites usable by people of all abilities and disabilities, following standards like **WCAG 2.1**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **industry standards** used throughout professional web design and are commonly referenced in KTU viva and design juries:
>
> - **Standard Desktop Resolution:** **1920 × 1080 pixels** (Full HD baseline)
> - **Standard Mobile Resolution:** **375 × 667 pixels** (iPhone baseline)
> - **Recommended Page Load Time:** **≤ 3 seconds** (Google Web Vitals threshold)
> - **Minimum Touch Target Size:** **44 × 44 CSS pixels** (Apple HIG / WCAG 2.5.5)
> - **Optimal Body Text Size:** **16 px** minimum for body copy
> - **Color Contrast Ratio (WCAG AA):** **4.5:1** for normal text, **3:1** for large text
> - **Maximum Recommended Line Length:** **50–75 characters** per line for readability

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Web Development Lifecycle (WDLC) — A 6-Phase Model

Building a website is a **systematic engineering and design process**, not a single act of "writing code." The KTU 2024 syllabus structures it into the following six phases:

### Phase 1 — Discovery & Strategy
- **Goal:** Understand *why* the website is being built and *who* it is for.
- **Activities:** Stakeholder interviews, user research, competitor analysis, defining KPIs (Key Performance Indicators), content audit, and establishing the **project brief**.
- **Output:** A *project charter* with goals, audience personas, success metrics, and budget.

### Phase 2 — Information Architecture (IA) & Planning
- **Goal:** Organize content so users can *find* what they need intuitively.
- **Activities:** Card sorting, sitemap creation, user flow mapping, content hierarchy definition, and taxonomy planning.
- **Output:** *Sitemap*, *user flows*, *content inventory*.

### Phase 3 — Design (UX + UI)
- **Goal:** Translate the IA into a *visual and interactive experience*.
- **Activities:** Wireframing (low-fidelity) → Mockups (high-fidelity) → Interactive prototyping → Design system creation (colors, typography, components).
- **Output:** *Style guide*, *UI mockups*, *interactive prototype* (Figma, Adobe XD, Sketch).

### Phase 4 — Development (Front-End + Back-End)
- **Goal:** Build a *functional* website from the approved designs.
- **Activities:** HTML structuring, CSS styling, JavaScript interactivity, back-end API development, database setup, CMS integration, third-party integrations.
- **Output:** *Working website* on a staging server.

### Phase 5 — Testing & Quality Assurance (QA)
- **Goal:** Verify that the website works *for everyone, everywhere, every time*.
- **Activities:** Functional testing, cross-browser testing, cross-device testing, accessibility audit (WCAG), performance testing (Core Web Vitals), security testing (OWASP), user acceptance testing (UAT).
- **Output:** *QA report*, *bug log*, *accessibility conformance report*.

### Phase 6 — Deployment, Launch & Maintenance
- **Goal:** Move the website to *production* and keep it healthy.
- **Activities:** DNS configuration, SSL certificate installation, production deployment, monitoring setup, analytics integration, content updates, iterative improvements.
- **Output:** *Live website* + *maintenance plan*.

## 2.2 The Technology Stack — Front-End, Back-End, and Database

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Although PECNT637 is a *design* course, you must understand the *technology stack layers* conceptually. You are **not** expected to code production apps, but you **must** be able to *diagram and justify* technology choices in your design portfolio.

### Front-End (Client-Side) — What runs in the browser:

| Technology | Role | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **HTML** (HyperText Markup Language) | Structure & content (skeleton) | The *bones* of the body |
| **CSS** (Cascading Style Sheets) | Presentation & layout (skin) | The *clothes* and *appearance* |
| **JavaScript (JS)** | Behavior & interactivity (muscle) | The *nervous system* that makes it move |

### Back-End (Server-Side) — What runs on the server:

| Component | Role | Example Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **Server** | Receives and responds to HTTP requests | Nginx, Apache |
| **Server-Side Language** | Executes business logic | Python (Django/Flask), Node.js, PHP, Ruby |
| **Database** | Stores and retrieves data | MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB |
| **API** | Bridge between front-end and back-end | REST, GraphQL |

## 2.3 The Client–Server Request–Response Model

Every interaction on a website is fundamentally a **request–response cycle**:

1. The user types a URL or clicks a link in their **browser (client)**.
2. The browser resolves the domain name to an IP address via **DNS**.
3. The browser sends an **HTTP/HTTPS request** to the server at that IP.
4. The **server** processes the request — fetching data from the database if needed.
5. The server sends back an **HTTP response** containing the requested resource (HTML, CSS, JS, images, or JSON data).
6. The browser **renders** the response into the visual webpage the user sees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for designers:** Understanding this cycle helps you design *loading states*, *error states*, *empty states*, and *skeleton screens* — all critical UX patterns for moments when the network is slow or fails.

## 2.4 Responsive Design — The Three Core Techniques

A website must look and work well on a **375 px phone**, a **768 px tablet**, a **1366 px laptop**, and a **1920 px desktop**. KTU 2024 expects you to know **three primary techniques** for achieving this:

### Technique 1 — Fluid Grids
Instead of fixed pixel widths, layouts use **relative units** (percentages, `fr`, `em`, `rem`, `vw`, `vh`) so columns expand and contract with the viewport.

### Technique 2 — Flexible Images
Images are sized with `max-width: 100%` so they scale down within their containers without overflowing, while `srcset` and the `<picture>` element serve different image resolutions to different devices (responsive images).

### Technique 3 — CSS Media Queries
Conditional CSS rules that apply different styles based on device characteristics (most commonly `min-width` and `max-width` breakpoints).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Building Websites" is a **design + technology** topic (not a pure mathematics topic), this section presents the **exhaustive step-by-step methodology** for designing and building a website, including the underlying logical derivations of the CSS Box Model, the responsive breakpoint logic, and the information architecture flow. Every transition is explicitly explained.

## 3.1 The Complete Website Building Workflow — 12 Exhaustive Steps

Below is the **end-to-end procedure** you would follow to build a professional website. Each step is expanded with the *why*, the *how*, and the *deliverable*.

### Step 1 — Define Purpose & Audience
- **Why:** A website without a clear purpose and audience is just decoration.
- **How:** Conduct stakeholder interviews, create **user personas** (fictional archetypal users), and write a **problem statement**.
- **Deliverable:** A 1-page *project brief* with purpose, audience, goals, and KPIs.

### Step 2 — Conduct Research & Competitive Analysis
- **Why:** You cannot design in a vacuum. You must understand the *landscape*.
- **How:** Audit 5–10 competitor websites, identify industry design patterns, document best practices, and list features you will adopt/reject.
- **Deliverable:** A *competitive audit matrix* (features × competitors).

### Step 3 — Build the Information Architecture (IA)
- **Why:** IA is the *skeleton* of the site — without it, navigation becomes chaotic.
- **How:** Perform **open card sorting** with users, group content into logical categories, and build a **sitemap** (a hierarchical tree of all pages).
- **Deliverable:** A *sitemap diagram* and a *content inventory spreadsheet*.

### Step 4 — Map User Flows
- **Why:** User flows visualize the *paths* a user takes to complete a task (e.g., "buy a product," "sign up," "find contact info").
- **How:** Identify the top 3–5 user tasks; for each, diagram the start → decisions → end states using flowchart notation.
- **Deliverable:** 3–5 *user flow diagrams*.

### Step 5 — Create Low-Fidelity Wireframes
- **Why:** Wireframes force you to think about *layout and hierarchy* before getting distracted by colors and fonts.
- **How:** Sketch (paper or digital with Balsamiq/Figma) grayscale boxes representing content blocks, navigation, CTAs (Call-To-Action), and image placeholders.
- **Deliverable:** *Wireframes* for the homepage and 3–5 key inner pages (mobile + desktop).

### Step 6 — Design the Visual System (Style Guide)
- **Why:** Consistency builds trust and brand recognition.
- **How:** Define a **design system** containing:
  - **Color palette:** primary, secondary, accent, neutral, semantic (success/warning/error) colors.
  - **Typography:** heading font, body font, size scale (e.g., 12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 32, 48 px).
  - **Spacing scale:** 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64 px (an 8-pt grid is the industry standard).
  - **Component library:** buttons, forms, cards, navigation patterns.
- **Deliverable:** A *style guide / design system document* in Figma.

### Step 7 — Build High-Fidelity Mockups
- **Why:** These are the *visual blueprints* handed to developers.
- **How:** Apply the style guide to the wireframes; add real content, images, and micro-interactions; create one mockup per unique page state.
- **Deliverable:** *High-fidelity mockups* for all key pages, desktop and mobile.

### Step 8 — Create an Interactive Prototype
- **Why:** A prototype lets you *test* the design before writing a single line of code.
- **How:** Link the mockups in Figma/Adobe XD with hotspots that simulate clicks, hovers, transitions, and form submissions.
- **Deliverable:** An *interactive prototype* that 5–8 users can complete tasks on.

### Step 9 — Conduct Usability Testing
- **Why:** Designers are not users. Real users will reveal blind spots.
- **How:** Run **moderated usability tests** (think-aloud protocol) with 5–8 representative users. Record where they hesitate, fail, or get confused. Compute the **task success rate** for each critical task.
- **Deliverable:** A *usability report* with findings and recommended changes.

### Step 10 — Iterate the Design
- **Why:** First designs are almost never the best designs.
- **How:** Apply the usability findings, refine the mockups, and re-test if changes are significant.
- **Deliverable:** *Revised mockups* (version 2.0).

### Step 11 — Develop (Build the Code)
- **Why:** The mockup must become a *living, breathing* website.
- **How:**
  - **Front-end:** Write semantic HTML5, style with CSS3 (using Flexbox and Grid for layout), add interactivity with JavaScript or a framework (React, Vue, Svelte).
  - **Back-end:** Set up the server, build the database schema, create APIs, integrate CMS (Content Management System) if needed (WordPress, Strapi, Sanity).
  - **Version control:** All code committed to a **Git** repository on **GitHub/GitLab**.
- **Deliverable:** A *staging website* (live URL not yet public).

### Step 12 — Test, Launch, and Maintain
- **Why:** Launch is the *beginning*, not the end.
- **How:**
  - **QA:** Cross-browser, cross-device, accessibility (axe, Lighthouse), performance (Lighthouse, WebPageTest), security (OWASP ZAP).
  - **Deploy:** Push to production server (Netlify, Vercel, AWS, DigitalOcean, traditional hosting).
  - **Monitor:** Set up Google Analytics, Search Console, error tracking (Sentry).
  - **Maintain:** Schedule content updates, security patches, and quarterly UX audits.
- **Deliverable:** *Live website* + *maintenance schedule*.

## 3.2 The CSS Box Model — Logical Derivation

The **CSS Box Model** is the foundation of all web layout. Every HTML element is rendered as a rectangular box with four concentric layers:

$$
\text{Total Element Width} = \text{Width} + 2 \times \text{Padding} + 2 \times \text{Border} + 2 \times \text{Margin}
$$

The same logic applies to height:

$$
\text{Total Element Height} = \text{Height} + 2 \times \text{Padding} + 2 \times \text{Border} + 2 \times \text{Margin}
$$

### Step-by-step derivation of the box model logic:

**Step 1 — Content (innermost layer)**
This is the actual text, image, or media inside the element. Its size is set by the `width` and `height` CSS properties.

**Step 2 — Padding (transparent space *inside* the border)**
Padding creates breathing room *between* the content and the border. It is part of the element's clickable/tappable area and inherits the element's background color.

**Step 3 — Border (the visible edge)**
A line (solid, dashed, dotted, or none) that wraps the padding and content. It can have a width, style, and color.

**Step 4 — Margin (transparent space *outside* the border)**
Margin creates distance *between* this element and its siblings. Unlike padding, margin is *not* part of the element — it is space between elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Box Sizing:** By default, `width` and `height` in CSS apply to the *content only* (the **content-box** model). This often surprises students. The modern best practice is to set `box-sizing: border-box;` globally, which makes `width` include padding and border, making layouts far more predictable.

## 3.3 Responsive Breakpoint Logic — Derivation

Modern web design follows a **mobile-first** philosophy. The logic is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Design for the smallest screen first (320–375 px width)**
Constraints: vertical space, single-column layout, hamburger menu, large touch targets, minimal text.

**Step 2 — Define breakpoints where the layout *breaks***
A breakpoint is a viewport width at which the current layout *no longer works well*. We use CSS media queries to add new styles at that width:

```css
/* Mobile-first base styles */
.container { width: 100%; padding: 16px; }

/* Tablet — 768px and up */
@media (min-width: 768px) {
  .container { width: 750px; margin: 0 auto; }
}

/* Desktop — 1024px and up */
@media (min-width: 1024px) {
  .container { width: 980px; display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; }
}

/* Large desktop — 1440px and up */
@media (min-width: 1440px) {
  .container { width: 1200px; }
}
```

**Step 3 — Test, measure, adjust**
Use Chrome DevTools device emulation, real-device testing (BrowserStack), and analytics to see which breakpoints your *actual users* use.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Tech Stack

When a designer must *recommend* a technology stack (a common KTU 2024 viva question), use the following decision matrix:

| Project Type | Front-End | Back-End | Database | Hosting | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Personal Portfolio** | HTML + CSS + minimal JS | None (static site) | None | GitHub Pages, Netlify | Free, fast, simple |
| **Small Business Site** | HTML + CSS + JS | WordPress (CMS) | MySQL | Managed WordPress host | Easy content updates |
| **E-Commerce Store** | React/Vue + Tailwind CSS | Node.js / Django / Laravel | PostgreSQL / MongoDB | AWS, Vercel, Shopify | Scalability, payment APIs |
| **Web Application (SaaS)** | React/Angular/Vue | Node.js/Python/Go | PostgreSQL | AWS, GCP, Azure | Performance, security, scale |
| **Blog / News Portal** | Static site generator (Next.js, Hugo) | Headless CMS (Strapi, Sanity) | MongoDB | Vercel, Netlify | Speed, SEO, content workflow |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the concept of Responsive Web Design (RWD) and list any three techniques used to achieve it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Responsive Web Design (RWD) is an approach to web design where a website's layout, images, and functionality adapt fluidly to the screen size, resolution, and orientation of the device being used, providing an optimal viewing experience across desktops, tablets, and mobile phones.

**Three core techniques of RWD:**

1. **Fluid Grids** — Layouts use relative units (percentages, `fr`, `vw/vh`) instead of fixed pixels, so columns expand and contract with the viewport.
2. **Flexible/Media Images** — Images use `max-width: 100%` and the `<picture>` element / `srcset` attribute to scale within their containers and serve appropriate resolutions.
3. **CSS Media Queries** — Conditional CSS rules (`@media (min-width: ...)`) apply different styles at defined breakpoints (e.g., 768 px, 1024 px).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any 2 techniques, 1 mark for the third technique or an example.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Differentiate between Front-End and Back-End development in web design. Give one example technology for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Front-End Development | Back-End Development |
|---|---|---|
| **Where it runs** | In the user's browser (client-side) | On a remote server (server-side) |
| **Purpose** | Structure, style, and interactivity visible to the user | Business logic, data processing, and storage |
| **Languages/Tech** | HTML, CSS, JavaScript | Python (Django/Flask), Node.js, PHP, Ruby |
| **Example** | **HTML** for page structure | **MySQL** for the database |

*(Award 1 mark each for the difference in 3 aspects: where it runs, what it does, and a technology example. Or 1.5 marks for the table + 1.5 marks for explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an *internal choice* — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the six phases of the Web Development Lifecycle (WDLC) in detail, with one deliverable for each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Client-Server Request-Response model. Draw a labeled diagram and explain what happens when a user types a URL into a browser. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Six Phases of WDLC (7 marks):**

1. **Discovery & Strategy (1 mark):** Understanding the *why* and the *who*. Activities include stakeholder interviews, user research, competitor analysis, and KPI definition. **Deliverable:** Project brief / project charter.
2. **Information Architecture & Planning (1 mark):** Organizing content so users can find it. Activities include card sorting, sitemap creation, and content inventory. **Deliverable:** Sitemap diagram.
3. **Design — UX/UI (1 mark):** Translating IA into visual and interactive designs. Activities include wireframing, mockups, and style guide creation. **Deliverable:** UI mockups + style guide.
4. **Development (1 mark):** Building the website using HTML, CSS, JavaScript (front-end) and server-side technologies (back-end). Activities include coding, version control (Git), and CMS integration. **Deliverable:** Working website on a staging server.
5. **Testing & QA (1 mark):** Verifying functionality, accessibility, performance, and cross-browser compatibility. Activities include usability testing, Lighthouse audits, and security scans. **Deliverable:** QA report.
6. **Deployment, Launch & Maintenance (2 marks):** Pushing to production, configuring DNS/SSL, integrating analytics, and scheduling content/security updates. **Deliverable:** Live website + maintenance plan.

**Part (b) — Client-Server Request-Response Model (7 marks):**

When a user types a URL (e.g., `https://www.ktu.ac.in`) into a browser, the following sequence occurs:

1. **URL Parsing (1 mark):** The browser parses the URL into protocol (`https`), domain (`www.ktu.ac.in`), and path.
2. **DNS Resolution (1 mark):** The browser queries a DNS server to translate the human-readable domain name into a machine-readable IP address (e.g., `203.0.113.42`).
3. **TCP Connection + TLS Handshake (1 mark):** The browser establishes a TCP connection to the server and (for HTTPS) performs a TLS handshake to encrypt the session.
4. **HTTP Request (1 mark):** The browser sends an HTTP request — typically a `GET` request — including headers (user-agent, cookies, accepted formats).
5. **Server Processing (1 mark):** The web server receives the request, the application logic executes (database queries, business rules), and the response is generated.
6. **HTTP Response (1 mark):** The server returns an HTTP response: a status code (e.g., `200 OK`) plus the body (HTML, CSS, JS, images, or JSON).
7. **Rendering (1 mark):** The browser parses the HTML, applies CSS, executes JavaScript, and paints the final visual webpage on the screen.

*(Award 1 mark per correct step. Diagram must include Browser, DNS, Web Server, Database, and arrows showing direction of request/response.)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** What is the CSS Box Model? With a neat labeled diagram, explain its four layers. Derive the formula for total element width. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the four main types of websites (static, dynamic, e-commerce, portfolio) with one real-world example for each. Compare their typical technology stacks. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — CSS Box Model (7 marks):**

The CSS Box Model is the foundational concept describing how every HTML element is rendered as a rectangular box composed of four concentric layers.

**The four layers (1 mark per layer, 4 marks total):**

1. **Content** — The innermost area containing the actual text, image, or media. Sized by `width` and `height`.
2. **Padding** — Transparent space *inside* the border, between the content and the border edge. Inherits the element's background.
3. **Border** — The visible edge wrapping padding and content. Can be solid, dashed, or dotted.
4. **Margin** — Transparent space *outside* the border, creating distance between this element and its siblings.

**Formula derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Total Width} = \text{Width}_{\text{content}} + 2 \times \text{Padding} + 2 \times \text{Border} + 2 \times \text{Margin}
$$

**Derivation steps:**

- Start with the content width: $W_c$.
- Add left + right padding: $W_c + P_l + P_r = W_c + 2P$.
- Add left + right border: $(W_c + 2P) + B_l + B_r = W_c + 2P + 2B$.
- Add left + right margin: $(W_c + 2P + 2B) + M_l + M_r = W_c + 2P + 2B + 2M$.

> **KTU Tip:** With `box-sizing: border-box`, the formula simplifies to:
> $$\text{Total Width} = \text{Specified Width} + 2 \times \text{Margin}$$
> because padding and border are absorbed into the specified width.

**Part (b) — Types of Websites (7 marks):**

| # | Type | Description | Example | Typical Tech Stack |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Static Website** (1.5 marks) | Fixed content; same for every visitor. No database or server-side processing. | A personal resume site, e.g., `johndoe.com` | HTML + CSS, GitHub Pages, Netlify |
| 2 | **Dynamic Website** (1.5 marks) | Content changes based on user, time, or interaction; pulls from a database. | KTU university portal `ktu.ac.in` (login + personalized dashboard) | WordPress, Node.js + React, MySQL |
| 3 | **E-Commerce Website** (2 marks) | Browse, search, add to cart, checkout, and pay for products online. | `amazon.in`, `flipkart.com` | React/Vue front-end, Node/Django back-end, PostgreSQL, Stripe/Razorpay payment API |
| 4 | **Portfolio Website** (2 marks) | Showcases a designer's or artist's work to attract clients or employers. | `behance.net` profile, `dribbble.com` | HTML/CSS/JS, often built on Webflow, Squarespace, or custom Figma-to-code |

*(Award marks for: clear type distinction, accurate example, correct tech stack, and a brief justification of why that stack fits.)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE for "Building Websites":**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Front-End" with "Back-End"** — A surprising number of students write "HTML is a back-end language." **It is not.** HTML is purely front-end/markup. Servers never execute HTML.
> 2. **Forgetting the iterative nature** — Writing the WDLC as a strict linear sequence. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes *iteration*; a phase 2 design change after user testing must flow *back* to phase 1 (research). Always mention *iteration*.
> 3. **Missing deliverables** — A common 1–2 mark deduction. Every phase *must* have a named deliverable (sitemap, wireframe, mockup, prototype, QA report, live site). Students who omit deliverables lose easy marks.
> 4. **Using `width` and `height` loosely** — In CSS questions, students often forget the box-sizing distinction. Always state which box model you are using (`content-box` vs `border-box`).
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams** — Part (b) of 14-mark questions **demands a labeled diagram**. A written answer without a diagram typically loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring accessibility and SEO** — In 2024, omitting WCAG/accessibility and SEO basics from a "build a website" answer is considered an *incomplete* answer. Always mention at least one accessibility consideration (contrast ratio, alt text, keyboard navigation).
> 7. **Outdated tools** — Mentioning Flash, FrontPage, or Dreamweaver as current tools will mark you as outdated. The 2024 industry standard is **Figma** for design and **Git + GitHub** for version control.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- [ ] A **website** is a collection of interlinked web pages served from one or more web servers under a common domain.
- [ ] Building a website is a **design + engineering process**, not just coding. Follow the 6-phase **WDLC**.
- [ ] The **web design process** = *Discover → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test → Deploy → Maintain* (iterative).
- [ ] **Front-End triad** = **HTML** (structure) + **CSS** (style) + **JavaScript** (behavior).
- [ ] **Back-End** = Server + Application Logic + **Database** + **APIs**.
- [ ] **Client-Server model** = Browser sends **HTTP request** → Server processes → Server returns **HTTP response** → Browser renders.
- [ ] **DNS** translates domain name → IP address. **HTTPS** = HTTP + TLS encryption (always use it in 2024).
- [ ] **Responsive Web Design (RWD)** uses 3 techniques: **Fluid Grids + Flexible Images + Media Queries**.
- [ ] **Mobile-first** is the modern standard. Common breakpoints: 320, 480, 768, 1024, 1280, 1440 px.
- [ ] **CSS Box Model** = Content + Padding + Border + Margin. Total width = W + 2P + 2B + 2M.
- [ ] `box-sizing: border-box` is the **modern best practice** — it makes layout math intuitive.
- [ ] **4 main types of websites**: Static, Dynamic, E-Commerce, Portfolio. Know examples for each.
- [ ] **Accessibility (WCAG 2.1)**: 4.5:1 contrast ratio for text, 44×44 px touch targets, alt text on all images, keyboard navigability.
- [ ] **Performance budget**: Page load ≤ 3 seconds. Use **Google Lighthouse** to measure.
- [ ] **SEO basics**: Semantic HTML, descriptive `<title>` and `<meta>` tags, alt text, fast load, mobile-friendly.
- [ ] **Design tools** (2024 standard): **Figma** for UI/UX, **Adobe XD** for prototypes, **Balsamiq** for wireframes.
- [ ] **Version control** is non-negotiable: **Git + GitHub/GitLab**.
- [ ] **Hosting options**: GitHub Pages (static), Netlify/Vercel (JAMstack), AWS/GCP/Azure (enterprise), shared hosting (legacy).
- [ ] Always perform **cross-browser**, **cross-device**, **accessibility**, **performance**, and **security** testing before launch.
- [ ] **Launch is the beginning, not the end** — monitor, update, and iterate continuously.
- [ ] The **3-click rule** is a usability heuristic: any page should be reachable within 3 clicks from the homepage.
- [ ] **Always include diagrams** in 14-mark answers — they are worth 2–3 marks by themselves.
- [ ] **Always mention iteration** — web design is *non-linear*; usability testing feeds back into redesign.
